Chartersofstructedtoinstituteproperproceedingsin theFranklin cir-

forfeited,&pro-suchcases,toforfeitthechartersofany railroad,turnpike,or

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

more than two years after its incorporation, to commence the construction of the roads or bridge, which, by their charters, they may have been authorized to make, or which, having commenced such work, shall have, for more than five years, ceased labor on the same without completing such road or bridge between the termini authorized by their charters: Provided, That where a specific period is fixed by the charter of such company for the commencement or completion of their road or bridge, no such proceedings shall be instituted until the period so fixed shall have expired.

§ 2. That whenever a forfeiture of the charter of any company shall have been adjudged in any proceeding under this act, it shall be the duty of the court to order a sale of all the rights and franchises, property and effects, of such company, upon such credits as to the court may seem equitable; but in no case shall the credit allowed be less than that allowed in the sale of real estate under the judgment of a court of equity. The purchasers shall give bond, with good security, for the payment of the several installments of the purchase money; and, in addition, the purchaser of the road-way shall give bond, with like security, conditioned that he will, within six months after the date of sale, resume work upon the road or bridge, and shall, with all reasonable dispatch, prosecute the work, so as that the same shall be completed between the termini fixed by the charter within five years from said date of sale, and thereupon such purchaser shall become vested with all the rights, privileges, and franchises which may have been conferred by law upon the original company, and shall be a body politic and corporate, by the same name and title as said original company, with the addition of the word (new) in brackets at the end of such title.

§ 3. That the proceeds of the sales of the property-rights and franchises of any company whose charter may have been so forfeited as aforesaid, shall be applied, first, to the payment of the costs of suit, including a fair and reasonable compensation to the Attorney General for his services in prosecuting the same, which compensation shall be fixed by the judge of said court at not less than two per cent. on the amount of sale, but not to exceed in any single case the sum of one thousand dollars; and, secondly, to the payment of any debts due or owing by said company; and the balance, if any there be, shall be paid over to the stockholders of said company in proportion to the amount of stock held by them respectively.

§ 4. That if the purchaser at any such sale shall fail to complete the work within the termini fixed by the charter, so as to have the same fit for the safe and speedy transportation of freight and passengers within the term of five years next after the date of his purchase, the property-rights and franchises so purchased by him, together with any improvements or additions thereto, shall be forfeited, and shall vest in the Commonwealth absolutely: Provided, however, That this act shall not apply to the Barren County railroad company.

§ 5. That this act shall take effect on the 1st day of July, 1866.

Approved January 30, 1866.

RESOLUTION inregardto General Fisk.
WHEREAS ,It isrepresentedin the publicjournalsthatMa-
jorGeneral ClintonB. Fisk,the head of the Freedmen's Bureau
in Kentucky ,did, on the 18th instant, deliveran addressin
the cityof Cincinnati,Ohio ,in which he made ,amongst oth-
ers, thesestatements: "Only the day beforeyesterday, in
Lexington , thirteendischarged coloredsoldiersstood in the
streets, in fullsightof Henry Clay's monument ,with their
bodies lacerated, theirbacks bleeding from the cruel lash,
theirheads cut tothe scalp,and one or two of them with their
eyes put out! and what for, do you suppose? Simply for
going to theirformer masters and asking fortheirwives and
children! I appealedtothe civilauthoritiesintheirbehalf,
but was told that therewas no law in Kentucky to help them .
I heard there of a slaveto be soldon a certainday . I made
an arrangement with the master commissioner to buy him
myself, as the lastslavetobe soldin Kentucky;" and whereas,
itisbelieved thatthosestatementshave no foundationin fact,
and are calculatedto place the peopleof Kentucky in a false
lightbefore the country; therefore,
Be itresolvedby theGeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky:
That a committee of two from the House and one from the
Senate be appointed,whose duty itshallbe to proceed imme-
diatelyto the cityof Lexington, and ascertainthe truth or
falsityof the statementsallegedto have been made by said
Fisk; and that saidcommitteenotifyGeneralFiskby mail
oftheirappointment,inorderthathe may appearbeforethe
committee if he so desire,and communicate to them the name
ofthe person by whom the truthof the statementmade by
him can be established,and the name of the civilofficerby
whom the opinionmentionedby him was given,and reportto
thisGeneral Assembly . ApprovedJanuary30,1866.

In consideration of a communication received from the
Provisional Governor of the State of Texas , with which he
transmittedtoHis Excellency,Thomas E Bramlette,Governor
of Kentucky , the flagof the 19th Kentucky volunteer in-
fantry,which was capturedby the Confederatesduring one of

Be itresolvedby the GeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :

fortune .

thanks of the GeneralAssembly and peopleof Kentucky ,

Maine to Texas,from theAtlanticto the Pacific.

## CHAPTER 5.

AN ACT to amend the charter of the Bank of Hopkinsville.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the capital stock of the Bank of Hopkinsville is hereby increased to the sum of three hundred thousand dollars, and the directors of said bank are authorized to sell the new stock at such time or times as in their judgment the interests of the bank and business of the community may require; but before selling the same, they are authorized to pay to the old stockholders such cash or stock dividend as in their judgment shall be equitable between the old and new stockholders: Provided, The capital stock shall not be impaired thereby.

§ 2. That section seven of the original act be repealed, and the following adopted in lieu thereof, viz.: That no greater cash dividend than ten per centum per annum shall be paid to the stockholders, unless the surplus fund remaining on land shall be equal to one tenth of the capital paid in.

§ 3. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved December 11, 1865.

## CHAPTER 6.

AN ACT to amend the charter of the “Argilite Mining and Manufacturing Company.”

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the name of the “Argilite Mining and Manufacturing Company” is hereby changed, and said corporation shall hereafter be known and styled the “Kentucky Improvement Company.”

Name changed:

§ 2. The said corporation is hereby authorized to hold its meetings without the limits of the Commonwealth, and to provide by suitable by-laws the proper mode of notifying and holding the same; meetings of the board of directors may also be held, conformably to established by-laws, without the limits of the Commonwealth; and transfer offices for the transferring of the stock, and the issuing of new certificates thereof, may be established in any city or cities of the United States.

May hold meetings out of the State &c.

§ 3. The said corporation is hereby authorized to increase its capital stock to the amount of one million of dollars, which

## CHAPTER 11.

AN ACT to incorporate the Baker Farm Oil Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That Samuel H. Grosvenor, William H. Phelps, Curtis G. Hussey, Nicholas Veeder, Herman Veeder, David M. Book, Thomas M. Howe, William R. Howe, and James Adair, their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and they are hereby, constituted a body corporate and politic, by the name and style of "Baker Farm Oil Company;" and by that name and style shall have perpetual succession, with power to contract and be contracted with; sue and be sued in that name, in all courts and places; to engage in mining and boring for petroleum or rock oil, salt water, and other valuable minerals, and in the manufacturing, refining, preparing for market, transporting, and selling oil and salt, and other products of said business, and of the lands now owned, or which may hereafter be owned by them in this State, and transport and sell their oil, salt, and other products within or without the State.

§ 2. When said corporators may wish to organize, any one or more of their number shall call a meeting, at such convenient time and place as may be designated by notice previously given, and they, the said corporators, or a majority of them, may organize by electing three or more directors, a president (who shall be one of the directors), and such other officers and managers as they may deem necessary; and when thus organized, the said company shall then have power, at such meeting or thereafter, to make such by-laws, rules and regulations, as they may deem necessary, from time to time, for the government and prosecution of the business of said corporation, not inconsistent with the Constitution and laws of the United States and of this State, and the same may alter, amend, or repeal at pleasure.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall be one hundred thousand dollars, and said company shall have power to increase the same, from time to time, not to exceed the amount of one million dollars.

§ 4. The said company may take, buy, lease, or rent any suitable lands, mines, oil and salt rights and privileges, rights of way, and other property necessary for their business, and may dispose of the same, or any portion thereof, by sale or

Digitized by Google

LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1865 . otherwise. They may receiverealestate,leaseholds,mining
and boring rights, and rightsof way and other property, in
payment of such part of subscriptionsas they may deem ad-
visable. They may erectand build, on any of theirlands,
such buildings,engines,machineryand fixtures, as may bedeemed convenientand properforcarryingon and conduct-
ing the business of said corporation .
§ 5. The saidcompany shallhave power to make and use a
common seal,and alterthe same at pleasure; to hold their
meetings within or without the State,and within the United
States,and shallhave allotherpowers needfuland proper for
the successfulprosecutionof theirbusiness,and forthe execu-
tion of the powers herein granted .
§ 6. Nothing in this charter shallbe so construed as to
confer banking privileges.
§ 7. This actshalltake effectfrom and afteritspassage.
Approved December 16,1865.

## CHAPTER 16.
1865.

AN ACT to incorporate the Kentucky Lubricating Oil Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That John F. Gunkel, George Vandyke, C. S. Cowan, John Wynn, H. A. Huntingdon, R. A. Bradshaw, E. D. Bannister, F. S. Sowers, their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and they are hereby, constituted a body corporate and politic, by the name and style of Kentucky Lubricating Oil Company; and by that name and style shall have perpetual succession, with power to contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued in that name, in all courts and places; to have a common seal; to engage in mining and boring for petroleum or rock oil, salt water, and other valuable minerals; and in the manufacturing and refining, preparing for market, transporting, and selling oil, salt, and other products of said business, and of the lands now owned, or which may hereafter be owned by them in this State; to hold their meetings, and transport and sell their oil, salt, and other products, within or without the State; and to have all other powers needful and proper for the successful prosecution of their business, and for the execution of the powers herein granted.

Corporators' names and corporate powers.

§ 2. That said corporators may organize said company by the appointment of a president, and such other officers and managers as they may deem necessary, at such time and place as they may designate by notice previously given; and when thus organized, the said company shall have power to make such by-laws, rules and regulations, as they may deem necessary, from time to time, for the government and prosecution of the business of said corporation, not inconsistent with the Constitution and laws of the United States and of this State.

When and how may organize.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall be three hundred thousand dollars, and said company shall have power to increase the same, from time to time, not exceeding six hundred thousand dollars.

Capital stock.

§ 4. The said company may buy, lease, or rent any suitable lands, mines, oil and salt rights and privileges, rights of way, and other property necessary for their business, and may dispose of the same, or any portion of it, by sale or otherwise. They may receive real estate, leaseholds, mining and boring rights, and rights of way, in payment of such part of subscriptions as they may deem advisable. Said company may erect and build, on any of their lands, such buildings, engines, machinery and fixtures, as may be deemed convenient and proper for carrying on and conducting the business of said corporation.

May buy or lease lands.

May erect buildings, &c.

§ 5. That this act shall take effect from and after its passage; and the right to repeal, alter, or amend this charter is reserved to the General Assembly; and no banking privileges are conferred by this act.

Approved December 16, 1865.

thereshouldbesome safeand reliableplaceofcrossingTrade-

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
33

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

1865.

§ 1. That a charter be granted to the Union and Crittenden toll bridge company of Union and Crittenden counties, Kentucky.

§ 2. That Samuel L. Casey, J. H. Rudy, Daniel A. Brooks, and N. W. Casey, together with their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and are hereby, appointed and made a corporation and body-politic, under the name and style of the Union and Crittenden toll-bridge company of Union and Crittenden counties, Kentucky; and by that name are made capable in law to have, receive, possess or purchase, enjoy to them and their successors, lands, tenements, goods, chattels of any kind, quality, or nature whatsoever, not to exceed at any one time the sum of twenty-five thousand dollars, and to sell and dispose of the same at any time or in any manner, and to sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, answer and be answered, defend and be defended, in any court of record or in any other place whatsoever; to put in execution such by-laws as may be necessary and convenient for the government of said corporation, not contrary to law.

§ 3. The capital stock of this company shall not exceed twenty-five thousand dollars, and shall be divided into shares of one hundred dollars each.

§ 4. The corporators of this act, or any five of them, shall, within twelve months from the passage of this law, open books for the subscription of stock, and when ten thousand dollars or more shall be subscribed (in shares of one hundred dollars), they shall give notice of a meeting of the subscribers, when twenty dollars shall be paid in hand on each share subscribed for; and the company shall be organized when the remaining eighty dollars on each share be secured, to be paid at the time and in the manner that shall be designated by the shareholders who have paid in twenty dollars on each share subscribed for.

§ 5. The corporators named, or any of them, who shall fail to subscribe for stock, shall cease to be members of the company; and all such subscribers as shall fail to pay twenty dollars on each share subscribed for on a day designated in the second call, shall cease to be stockholders, and the stock subscribed for by them may be subscribed for by others, under the direction of the person or persons who received the original subscriptions; and in case any subscriber shall fail to pay any call made by the board of directors (who are hereby authorized to make such calls) for the unpaid balance of his subscription, and remain in default for sixty days, the board may, in their discretion, forfeit his stock for the benefit of the company.

§ 6. The business of this company shall be managed by five directors, to be chosen by the subscribers, and each director shall hold at least one share; an election for directors shall be held at the first meeting of the subscribers; and the directors, when elected, shall continue in office until the first Monday in November next succeeding, or until their successors are

The capital stock, moneys and personal estate of said corporation, may be invested at the discretion of the directors, either in loans upon bonds and mortgages, upon real estate, or in United States stocks, bank stocks, or stock or bonds created by any State or of corporations created by this State; and the same may be called in and reinvested at pleasure, under the provisions of this act; and it shall be the duty of said corporation to make annually a report to the Auditor containing a full and accurate statement of its condition and affairs.

§ 11. Suits at law may be maintained by any stockholder, or person insured by said company, against said corporation, for losses or injuries insured against by said company, if payment shall be withheld more than thirty days after the same shall be due and payable by the terms of the policy of insurance, or other contract, and after the said corporation shall have been duly notified of such loss or injury.

§ 12. This act may be altered, amended, or repealed, at the pleasure of the General Assembly; and nothing contained therein shall be so construed as to authorize said company to engage in the business of banking.

§ 13. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved December 20, 1865.

The affairs of the corporation shall be managed by seven trustees, of whom the president of the company shall be one, and all of whom shall be stockholders in said corporation; their term of service shall be for one year, or until their successors are elected. Within a period not exceeding six months after the time when one hundred thousand dollars of said stock is subscribed, and ten per cent. thereof paid in, notice shall be given of the time and place of holding an election for a board of trustees and president, who shall be elected by a majority of interest of the stockholders represented. If any of those elected trustees or the president shall refuse or decline to act, or resign after acceptance, a majority of those accepting may fill the vacancy until the next election by the stockholders. The trustees, from time to time, may adopt, alter, repeal, and amend such by-laws and rules, for the general management of the property, business, and affairs of the company, as they may deem proper, not inconsistent with the laws and Constitution of this State; they may keep their offices at such place or places as they may deem best for the interest of the corporation; they may appoint and remove a secretary, treasurer, superintendent, and such other officers as they may deem necessary, with such compensation for services as they may fix; they may make such call of payment of subscriptions for stock as they may deem proper, not to exceed twenty per cent. for every thirty days; and in default of payment, the by-laws of said company may authorize a forfeiture of stock; they may use a common seal, and alter the same at pleasure; and they may require a bond, with security, from such officers as may be elected or appointed. Until the election hereinbefore provided for, the powers and functions vested in said board of trustees are vested in said corporators.

porate and politic, may or can do; and may have a common seal, which they may alter or break at pleasure, and may purchase, hold, and convey any estate, real or personal, for the use of said company: *Provided*, The said corporation shall not at any one time hold real estate exceeding in value five thousand dollars, excepting such as be taken for debt or held as collateral security for money due said company.

§ 2. This company may make insurance on all description of property against loss or damages by fire or lightning, wind, and the risks of inland navigation and transportation, or any other risks on property they may deem proper; and may cause themselves to be reimbursed upon the whole or any part of any risk on which they may have insurance.

§ 3. All the incorporate powers of the said company shall be exercised by a board of directors, consisting of five persons, all of whom shall be citizens of the United States; the directors shall hold their office two years, and until others are elected; they shall elect from their own body a president, vice president, and secretary, who shall each respectively hold office during the aforesaid term of two years, and until others are elected; but nothing herein shall be so construed as to prevent a director or other officer from being again eligible. Three members of the said board of directors shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business. The persons named in the first section of this act shall constitute the first board of directors, and their term of office shall commence at the time of organization of the company: *Provided*, In case of the death or resignation of the president or any of the directors, the vacancy may be filled by the board of directors. It shall and may be lawful, on any day, to make and hold an election of directors, in such manner as shall be directed by the by-laws of said company.

§ 4. The secretary may appoint an assistant secretary, whose duties shall be set forth in his certificate of appointment. It shall be the duty of the directors of said company to prescribe the duties of their respective officers and agents, and fix their compensation, and take such security from them as they may deem necessary for the faithful performance of their respective duties; they shall also determine the rates of insurance. The directors may extend the insurance of said company to any part of this State, or any other State or States.

§ 5. The rates of insurance shall be from time to time fixed and regulated by the company, and premium notes therefor shall be received from the insured, which shall be paid at such time or times, and in such sum or sums, as the company shall from time to time require for losses and expenses. Any person applying for insurance so electing, may pay a cash premium in addition to the premium note or a definite sum in money, to be fixed by said company, in full for said insurance in lieu of premium note.

§ 11. The board of directors may invest and employ the funds of said company in such a way and manner as the interests and welfare of the company may require: Provided, That nothing herein construed as authorizing them to engage in banking operations, or to traffic in any goods, wares, or merchandise, or to exempt any of the property of said company from taxation, except the premium notes.

§ 12. Said company may make insurance for any term not exceeding five years; and any policy of insurance issued by said company, signed by the president and countersigned by the secretary, shall be deemed valid and binding on said company. All cases where the assured has title in fee simple, unincumbered, to the building or buildings insured, and to the land covered by the same; but if the assured has a less estate herein, or if the premises unincumbered, the policy shall be void, unless the true title of the assured and the incumbrance on the premises be expressed therein.

§ 13. The said company may make and establish and put in execution such by-laws, ordinances, and regulations as shall, in their opinion, be necessary for the good government and management of their affairs of said company, and which are not repugnant to the laws and Constitution of this State and the United States.

§ 14. The business of the said company shall be carried on in the city of Louisville, State of Kentucky, or elsewhere, by agencies, as the directors shall direct, and at such agencies as they may establish: Provided also, That the home office can be removed to any other town or city in the State of Kentucky, by a unanimous vote of the directors of said company, also by giving notice to all members of the said company.

§ 15. Each member of said company shall be allowed one vote for each policy or policies, either by person or proxy duly appointed, only at elections for directors.

§ 16. Directors may vote by proxy duly appointed, and said proxy to remain on file in the office of said company for inspection of any member of said company. Each and every member of said company shall be entitled to and allowed an examination of the books, papers, and general transactions of said company, upon application thereof to the secretary.

§ 17. If insurance on any house or buildings, household furniture, merchandise, or other property, shall be, and subsist in said company, and in other office, or from and by any other person or persons, at the same time this insurance made in and by this company, shall be deemed and become void; unless such double insurance subsist by and with the consent of said company, and signed by indorsement on the books of the policy signed by the president or secretary.

§ 18. The said company may appoint an executive committee from their own members, who shall possess all the powers of said board of directors, when said board is not in session; said committee's duties shall be set forth in by-laws of said company.

CHAPTER 67 .
AN ACT forthebenefitof Muldrough'sHillturnpikeroad.
WHEREAS , The Stateisthe owner of that part of the turn-
pike road which passesfrom Newmarket up and over Mul-
drough'sHill,which isunder the charge and superintendence
of J.H. Chandler,and theexpensesforrepairsindispensably

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 57

necessary to be done having exceeded the tolls collected by the sum of two thousand dollars, which is now due; and, in order to pay the same,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That J. H. Chandler, the superintendent of said turnpike road, be, and he is hereby, authorized to borrow the sum of two thousand dollars, at an interest of not exceeding six per cent., for a period of not more than two years, and that he pay the same, with interest, out of the receipts from time to time of said road.

## CHAPTER 68.

AN ACT for the benefit of John A. Yandell, late sheriff of Crittenden county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That John A. Yandell, late sheriff of Crittenden county, have the further time of one year in which to collect and pay over the militia tax for the year 1862, and in which to make out and return a delinquent list, which he may do at any term of the Crittenden county court, in like manner, and under the like restrictions, as are now required by law: *Provided*, That his sureties in his official bond shall give their consent thereto in open court, which consent shall be made matter of record in said court.

§ 2. That said Yandell, sheriff as aforesaid, shall be released from the payment of all the interest which has accrued upon a judgment rendered against him in the Franklin circuit court at its June term, 1863, for the revenue of Crittenden county for the year 1862, whether it accrued before or after the rendition of said judgment; and the Auditor of Public Accounts shall enter the credit therefor on said judgment as if the same had been paid on the first day of June, 1862.

§ 3. This act shall be in force from its passage.

Approved December 20, 1865.

---

§ 5. That all fines and forfeitures collected under the provisions of this act shall be paid into the treasury of the city, and held as a fund as aforesaid for the purchase and repair of engines or other apparatus for the extinguishment of fires, or for defraying necessary expenses of the fire department.

§ 6. That it shall not be lawful for any person hereafter to erect within the limits of said city any wood or frame building, unless the same shall first be allowed by order of the board of council, upon application for that purpose; and should any person or persons, in violation of such covenant, erect any such building, it may be lawful for said board of councilmen of said city to cause the same to be removed at the expense of the owner of the ground upon which the same may be erected, which may be collected by suit before the proper tribunal, and, when collected, to be paid into the city treasury.

§ 7. That all laws and parts of laws which requires the ordinances of said city to be published in the "paper of the Public Printer," or any other newspaper, be, and the same is hereby repealed, and in lieu thereof, that the same shall be hereafter published by advertisement, to be placed at the courthouse door and at the market-house, or by delivery of a copy of any ordinance or notice upon the party required to make any improvement, repair, or comply with any order or ordinance, which shall be sufficient.

§ 8. That all acts or parts of acts now in force in relation to said city, which curtails the original boundary of that part of said city known as South Frankfort, be, and the same is hereby repealed.

§ 9. That this act take effect from and after its passage.

Approved December 20, 1865.

§ 2. The corporation is hereby authorized and empowered to purchase, sell, hold, and dispose of lands in fee simple, and by lease; of mining for coal and other minerals; and of boring and mining for petroleum or oils of whatever kind; and to refine, transport, and vend the same.

§ 3. The capital stock of the corporation shall be divided into shares of not less than five nor more than fifty dollars each, which shares may be issued and transferred in such manner, and upon such conditions, and under such rules of forfeiture for non-payment of calls and assessments, as the board of directors of such corporation may direct; and the liability of share-holders shall extend to, and be limited by, the amount of stock held by them respectively.

§ 4. The affairs of said corporation shall be managed by five or more directors, one of whom shall be president, and all of whom shall be stockholders. The first board of directors shall consist of Wm. Heilman, John Laval, John Schubert, Chas. Wolff, F. W. Cook, Christian Decker, Philip Decker, P. C. Decker, Albert Steinback, and C. Kraimer; and they shall continue in office for one year, or until their successors are elected by a majority in interest of said stockholders of said corporation, which corporation may exist in perpetuity; and the directors thus elected, and their successors in perpetual succession, shall continue in office for one year, or until their successors shall have been elected, and shall have entered upon their duties. If any of the above named directors shall decline or refuse to act, then a majority of those remaining shall have power to fill such vacancy. They may adopt such by-laws and rules for the government of the corporation and the management of its affairs as they may deem proper, not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of this State nor of the United States. The said corporators, or any of them, may open books for, and receive subscriptions to, the capital stock of said company hereby corporated, at such times and places, upon such notice, and coupled with such conditions, as the board of directors of said corporation may determine. The board of directors may fill vacancies in their body occasioned by resignation, death, or otherwise; they may make such calls for the payment of stock as they may deem proper; and, in the event of non-payment, may forfeit the stock of the defaulting shareholder, or collect the same by suit as they may elect. Right of way is hereby granted to said company over any lands, and on and along any water-courses within the State, with power and authority to condemn the same to their use in the same manner, and subject to such restrictions, as are and have been imposed by the general laws of the State of Kentucky. They shall have power and authority to construct and operate over the right of way thus granted common and turnpike roads, and to build and maintain on and along such water-courses locks and dams, and other fixtures and appliances for slackwater navigation to and from and between the site or sites of their mines and oil wells and the markets for the sale and storage of their products, and to and from and between such mines or

CHAPTER 77 .
AN ACT to incorporatethe " Cumberland River and Crocus Creek Of
Company ."
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth o
Kentucky :
§1. That William Terry, G. T. Whitney , T. C. Coleman
W. A. Klesendorff, and R. B. Hall,and their successors,be
and theyarehereby,createda body-corporateand politic,by
the name of " The Cumberland River and Crocus Creek Of
Company ," fortheterm of thirtyyears,with allthepoweri

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
67
1866.

and authority incident to corporations, for the purposes hereinafter mentioned.

§ 2. The corporation is hereby authorized and empowered to purchase and hold lands, in fee simple and by lease, for mining and boring for petroleum and other minerals, and to refine and vend the same.

§ 3. The capital stock of "The Cumberland River and Crocus Creek Oil Company" shall be one million dollars, and shall be divided into shares of not less than five nor more than one hundred dollars each, and may be issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions as the board of directors of said corporation may direct; and the capital stock of said company may be decreased or increased by the board of directors if deemed necessary.

§ 4. The affairs of said company shall be managed by five directors, one of whom shall be president, all of whom shall be stockholders in said corporation. The first board of directors shall consist of William Terry, G. T. Whitney, T. C. Coleman, W. A. Klesendorff, and R. B. Hall, who shall continue in office until their successors are elected by a majority in interest of the stockholders of said corporation; and the board of directors chosen by the stockholders shall continue in office for one year, or until their successors are elected. If any of the above named directors shall decline or refuse to act, a majority of the others shall fill the vacancy by appointing some one else. They may adopt such by-laws, rules and regulations, for the government of the corporation and management of its affairs and business as they may deem proper, and not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of the State and United States. The said corporators, or any of them, may open book of subscriptions, and receive subscriptions to the capital stock of said company herein incorporated, and books of subscription may be opened, and subscriptions received at such times and places, and upon such notices thereof, as any three of said incorporators may deem right and proper.

§ 5. The said "Cumberland River and Crocus Creek Oil Company" shall not own, in fee simple or by lease, lands exceeding in value five hundred thousand dollars. Notice shall be given of the time and place of election of a new board of directors, who shall hold office for one year, as provided herein. The board of directors of said corporation may fill vacancies occasioned by death or resignation or otherwise, and may make such calls of payments of stock as they deem proper, not to exceed twenty per cent. for every thirty days; they may keep their office at such place or places as they deem to the interest of the corporation. They may appoint a secretary, treasurer, superintendent, and other officers as they may deem necessary, with such compensation for services as they may fix; and by their by-laws regulate and fix the mode of keeping their records as may be deemed necessary.

§ 6. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved January 12, 1866.

## CHAPTER 79.

AN ACT to incorporate the Woodford County Agricultural and Mechanical Association.

WHEREAS, It is represented to the General Assembly that the citizens of the county of Woodford are anxious to create an association under the name and style of the Woodford County Agricultural and Mechanical Association, for the purpose of promoting improvements in all the various branches of agriculture, including not only the great staples of industry and trade, but also fruits, vegetables, and ornamental gardening, the promotion of the mechanic arts in all their branches, the improvement of the race of all useful and domestic animals, the general advancement of rural economy and household manufactures, and the dissemination of useful knowledge upon those subjects, and for that purpose have chosen a president, vice president and sixteen directors, also a secretary and treasurer of said association. and desire an act of incorporation; therefore,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That Frank P. Kinkead, president of said association, and J. W. Macelwain, vice president, Warren Viley, R. A. Alexander, W. J. Steele, D. J. Williams, W. A. Dunlap, James R Stephenson, James P. Ford, James R. Gay, Logan Railey, Robert McConnell, John Stout, J. W. Arnold, Robert Garrett, W. H. Graddy, James McKee, and Mason R. Henry, directors of said association, be, and they are hereby, created a body-politic and corporate, with perpetual succession, under the name and style of the "Woodford County Agricultural and Mechanical Association;" and by that name may sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, defend and be defended, in all courts of law and equity; and shall be capable of acquiring, by purchase or otherwise, any quantity of land in the county of Woodford, in the neighborhood of Versailles, not exceeding fifty acres. and may improve, sell, and convey the same, or any part thereof, at pleasure, and may acquire, hold, and dispose of such personal estate as the president and directors of said association may deem necessary and proper; the said association may adopt and use a corporate seal, or may use the private seal of its president for the time being.

§ 2. That the capital stock of said association shall be divided into shares of twenty-five dollars each, to be subscribed for and taken, including the shares already taken and subscribed, agreeable to such rules and regulations as the corpora-

## CHAPTER 89.

AN ACT to amend the road law in Mason county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky*:

§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved January 12, 1866.

## CHAPTER 100.
1866.

AN ACT for the benefit of Thomas Conway, late sheriff of Union county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

That the further time of two years, from and after the passage of this act, be allowed Thomas Conway, late sheriff of Union county, in which to list and collect his uncollected fee bills and taxes, subject to all the penalties now in force against the listing and collecting illegal fee bills, and said uncollected fee bills and taxes shall have distrainable force for the two years aforesaid.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

## CHAPTER 101.

AN ACT for the benefit of John G. Clagett, late sheriff of Grayson county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That John G. Clagett, late sheriff of Grayson county, be allowed the further time of two years to collect his arrearages of fee bills, revenue taxes, and county levy, and may distrain therefor; he, and his securities in his official bond, to be subject to the penalties imposed by law for the illegal collection of revenue and fee bills.

§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved January 12, 1866.

## CHAPTER 102.

AN ACT to repeal an act to create a soldiers' relief fund for Bourbon county, approved August 31st, 1862.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That an act, entitled "An act to create a soldiers' relief fund for Bourbon county," approved August 31, 1862, be, and the same is hereby, repealed.

§ 2. And it is hereby further enacted, that the sheriff of said county shall pay to the treasurer thereof all taxes he shall hereafter collect under said act, in virtue of the order of the last levy court of said county; and said treasurer shall apply the money so paid, under the directions of the commissioners of said county, in payment of the interest on the bonds issued by said county.

§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved January 12, 1866.

AN ACT creating an additional voting place in Lovelaceville precinct, Ballard county.

## CHAPTER 109.
AN ACT creating an additional voting place in Larue county.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*
§ 1. That all that part of Larue county lying north of line beginning on Middle creek, at Morgan Daugherty's, and running a straight line to George Hargun's, thence to C. F. Duncan's, thence to John Roberts', thence a straight line to the Rolling Fork below Frank Johnson's, leaving those mentioned on the line to choose their place of voting, shall be, and the same is hereby, made a voting district, with two justices of the peace and a constable. The two justices of the peace shall be nominated by the county court, and commissioned by the Governor; the constable shall be appointed by the county judge, who shall take of him the necessary bond. They shall hold their offices until the next general election, which shall be held for the time at James Edlin's.
§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.
Approved January 13, 1866.

That said lodge shall have power to pass such by-laws, rules and regulations, not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of this Commonwealth, as may be necessary for the safe-keeping of the property and other interests of said corporation; and may have and use a common seal, and change the same at pleasure.

Approved January 13, 1866.

## CHAPTER 118.

AN ACT to incorporate the “Confidence Oil Company.”

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That W. L. McCampbell, J. T. Boyle, S. S. Bush, J. M. Bryant, and W. A. Klesendorff, and their successors, be, and they are hereby, created a body corporate and politic, by the name of the “Confidence Oil Company,” for the term of thirty years, with all the powers and authority incident to corporations, for the purposes hereinafter mentioned.

§ 2. The corporation is hereby authorized and empowered to purchase and hold lands, in fee simple and by lease, for mining and boring for petroleum and other minerals, and to refine and vend the same.

§ 3. The capital stock of the “Confidence Oil Company” shall be one million dollars, and shall be divided into shares of not less than five nor more than one hundred dollars each, and may be issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions as the board of directors of said corporation may direct; and the capital stock of said company may be decreased or increased by the board of directors, if deemed necessary.

in interestof the stockholdersof said corporation; and the

CHAPTER 119 .
AN ACT to incorporatethe Greasy and Crocus Creek OilCompany .
Be itenactedbytheGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1.ThatWilliamL.McCampbell,J.T. Boyle,S.S.Bush,J.M. Bryant,and W. A. Klesendorff,and theirsuccessors,be,
and theyarehereby,createda body corporateand politic,bythename ofthe"Greasyand CrocusCreekOilCompany,"forthetermofthirtyyears,withallthepowersand authority
incidentto corporations,forthe purposeshereinaftermen-
tioned .
§ 2.The corporationisherebyauthorizedand empoweredtopurchaseand holdlands,infeesimpleand by lease,forminingand boringforpetroleumandotherminerals,andto
refine and vend the same .

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

§ 3. The capital stock of the Greasy and Crocus Creek Oil Company shall be one million dollars, and shall be divided into shares of not less than five nor more than one hundred dollars each, and may be issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions as the board of directors of said corporation may direct; and the capital stock of said company may be decreased or increased by the board of directors if deemed necessary.

§ 4. The affairs of said company shall be managed by five directors, one of whom shall be president, all of whom shall be stockholders in said corporation. The first board of directors shall consist of W. L. McCampbell, J. T. Boyle, S. S. Bush, J. M. Bryant, and W. A. Klesendorff, who shall continue in office until their successors are elected by a majority in interest of the stockholders of said corporation; and the board of directors chosen by the stockholders shall continue in office for one year or until their successors are elected. If any of the above named directors shall decline or refuse to act, a majority of the others shall fill the vacancy by appointing some one else. They may adopt such by-laws, rules and regulations, for the government of the corporation and management of its affairs and business, as they may deem proper, not inconsistent with the Constitution and laws of the State and United States. The said corporators, or any of them, may open book of subscriptions and receive subscriptions to the capital stock of said company herein incorporated, and books of subscription may be opened and subscriptions received at such times and places, and upon such notices thereof, as any three of said incorporators may deem right and proper.

§ 5. The said Greasy and Crocus Creek Oil Company shall not own, in fee simple or by lease, lands exceeding in value five hundred thousand dollars. Notice shall be given of the time and place of election of a new board of directors, who shall hold office for one year as provided herein. The board of directors of said corporation may fill vacancies occasioned by death or resignation or otherwise, and may make such calls of payments of stock as they deem proper, not to exceed twenty per cent. for every thirty days; they may keep their office at such place or places as they deem to the interest of the corporation; they may appoint a secretary, treasurer, superintendent, and other officers, as they may deem necessary, with such compensation for services as they may fix, and by their by-laws regulate and fix the mode of keeping their records as may be deemed necessary.

§ 6. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved January 13, 1866.

## CHAPTER 123.

AN ACT to incorporate the Minnehaha Oil and Mining Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That William F. Barret, W. P. Ledwick, W. Krippenstaple, John Ledwick, John Roberts, and John F. Tolen, and their successors, be, and they are hereby, created a body corporate and politic, by the name of the "Minnehaha Oil and Mining Company," for the term of thirty years, with all the powers and authority incident to corporations, for the purposes hereinafter mentioned.

## CHAPTER 125.

AN ACT for the benefit of G. S. Jones, sheriff of Marshall county.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That G. S. Jones, sheriff of Marshall county, be, and he is hereby, allowed the further time until the 1st day of June, 1866, to collect and pay over the revenue of said county, and the further time till the 1st day of June, 1866, to make out and return his delinquent list to the county court of said county: Provided, That the sureties upon his bond for the collection of the revenue shall appear before the county court of said county and give their consent to said extension.

§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved January 13, 1866.

## CHAPTER 127.

AN ACT to incorporate the Phoenix Oil and Mineral Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That D. F. Wilkin, John Lumsden, James Chamberlin, Henry Haynes, and Player Martin, their associates and successors, are hereby constituted a body-politic and corporate, with the right of succession, by the name of the Phoenix Oil and Mineral Company, for the purpose of exploring, boring, digging, and working for copper, lead, zinc, gold, silver, iron, coal, rock oil, petroleum, salt, and other ores, metals, minerals, oils, and liquids; and for such purposes may erect all necessary buildings, furnaces, engines, machinery, depots, and other apparatus and fixtures for carrying on the operations of mining, working, smelting, assaying, refining, and vending the same; and by that name may sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, appear, prosecute, and defend in any court of law or equity in all suits and actions; may have a common seal, the same alter and renew at pleasure; and may enjoy all the privileges incident to corporations; and may purchase, hold, own, mortgage, lease, and transfer and convey any real and personal estate.

§ 2. That the persons hereinbefore incorporated, or a majority of them, may organize said company by electing a board of directors, to consist of five members, who shall elect one of their number president; shall have the power to elect all other officers necessary to carry out the purposes of this corporation; fix salaries, and generally to act for the company. That said board shall continue in office such time as the persons electing the same may determine, not exceeding one year, and until their successors are elected by the stockholders; to adopt such by-laws for the government of the affairs of said company as to them may seem meet and proper, not inconsistent with the laws of the United States and of this State.

doubt; but no person or creditor shall have a lien on any money due or becoming due which has been contracted after premiums have been paid.

§ 11. No officer, agent, or stockholder, or policy-holder, can (except as hereinafter provided for policy-holders) borrow money, either directly or indirectly, from this company; nor can this company transact any other business than a legitimate life assurance business as herein granted. The company is permitted to invest its funds and surplus in such way and manner as will be most secure, safe, and most remunerative.

> No officer to borrow money of company.

§ 12. Suits at law may be maintained by this company against any of its members, and suits may be maintained by any of its members against the company; and in any suit between the company and any of its members, any member shall be admitted as a competent witness on behalf of the company.

> May maintain suits at law.

§ 13. The company is empowered to require all premiums to be paid in cash in advance, or may, when the policy-holder does not pay the usual mutual or stock rates of premiums, receive a note, or charge in account against such policy-holder the residue not paid in cash or covered by note; and in such cases the person acting as secretary shall keep a correct account, and the certificate of the secretary, approved and signed by the president, shall be *prima facie* evidence of the amount of indebtedness of the said policy-holder, which shall be a proper credit against the policy as a claim in any suit at law in any court of record in this Commonwealth or anywhere else.

> May require premiums to be paid in cash.

§ 14. It is hereby declared to be unlawful to use any of the capital stock for current expenses, except the claims of policy-holders; and a violation of this section is hereby declared a misdemeanor, punishable by fine or imprisonment, or both, at the discretion of the court having jurisdiction and trying the case.

> Capital stock not to be used for current expenses.

§ 15. The stock surplus, and all sums which may be set apart, shall be liable for the claims of policy-holders; but no stockholder shall be liable beyond his stock, nor policy-holder beyond his premium.

> Surplus fund liable for debts of corporation.

§ 16. Applications for insurance shall state all the material facts and circumstances affecting the risks, and no contract shall be binding on the company unless this is complied with.

> Applications for insurance.

§ 17. It shall be lawful for this company to reinsure any risk or any part thereof on which they have made insurance.

> May remove risks.

§ 18. It is declared to be the true meaning of section nine of this act that annuities granted by this company, and paid for previously, shall be free from the claims of creditors whose claims are contracted after the annuity is paid for and granted.

> Annuities to be free from claims of creditors.

§ 19. If the annuitant is over sixty years of age, it is hereby declared that the annuity cannot be transferred or sold by him or any other person, nor can it be purchased; and such sales or purchases are declared to be void, and the annuity shall be paid to the annuitant.

> If over sixty years of age, annuity cannot be transferred.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

books, as well as the stock and other books of said company, shall at all times be open to the inspection of any stockholder; the shares of said company shall be deemed and held as personal property.

§ 4. The affairs of said company shall be conducted by a board of directors not exceeding five in number, one of whom shall be president of the company, all of whom shall be stockholders. Said board shall consist of the corporators herein, who shall continue in office until their successors are elected by a majority of the stockholders in interest. The board of directors shall be elected annually, and shall continue in office one year or until their successors are duly elected; and in case of vacancy produced from any cause, the remaining directors shall have power to supply such vacancies, from time to time, as they may occur.

§ 5. The corporators herein may meet at such time and place as they may deem proper, and a majority being present, may organize said company, and receive subscriptions of stock in lands, leases, and other property, and proceed with the business of the company; may make by-laws, rules and regulations, for the thorough and efficient organization thereof; may direct the issuing of stock, determine the amount thereof, receive therefor the payment, affix the value of lands, leases, and other property received in payment for the stocks; and they and their successors may appoint all necessary officers and agents for conducting the business thereof; shall select a secretary and treasurer, define their duties, may require bond of them for faithful performance of their duties, and may fix the compensation for the services of all such officers and agents; may prescribe the mode in which the books and papers of said company shall be kept, and do all other acts necessary for the successful working of the business of the company.

§ 6. The principal office shall be at the city of Frankfort, subject to removal by order of the directors; may have branch offices at any other points which said board may designate.

§ 7. Each stockholder shall have as many votes as he has shares in said company; may vote by proxy or otherwise; they shall annually elect their directors, and the secretary shall give twenty days' notice (published in some newspaper in Kentucky) of the time and place of said election.

§ 8. No banking privileges are allowed by this charter, nor shall the directors have power to establish any by-law or regulation inconsistent with the Constitution or laws of the United States or of the State of Kentucky.

§ 9. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved January 16, 1866.

## CHAPTER 132.

AN ACT to incorporate the Eclipse Petroleum and Mining Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That D. C. Robinson, W. W. Bailey, A. Armel, T. T. Williamson, James McLelland, J. H. D. Rogers, George Shannon, O. Mulvey, A. Dunning, and their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and they are hereby, constituted a body corporate and politic, by the name and style of “Eclipse Petroleum and Mining Company;” and by that name and style shall have perpetual succession, with power to contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued in that name, in all courts and places; to have a common seal; to engage in mining and boring for petroleum, rock oil, salt water, and other valuable minerals, and in the manufacturing, refining, and preparing for market, transporting and selling oil, salt, and other products of said business, and of the lands now owned, or which may hereafter be owned by them in this State; to hold their meetings, and transport and sell their oil, salt, and other products within or without this State; and to have all powers needful and proper for the successful prosecution of their business, and for the execution of the powers herein granted.

§ 2. That said corporators may organize said company by the appointment of a president, and such other officers and managers they may deem necessary, at such time and place as they may designate by notice previously given; and when thus organized, the said company shall have power to make such by-laws, rules and regulations, as they may deem necessary, from time to time, for the government and prosecution of the business of said corporation, not inconsistent with the Constitution and laws of the United States and of this State.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall be one hundred and twenty thousand dollars, and said company shall have power to increase the same, from time to time, not exceeding five hundred thousand dollars.

§ 4. The said company may buy, lease, or rent any suitable lands, mines, oil and salt rights and privileges, rights of way, and other property necessary for their business, and may dispose of the same, or any portion thereof, by sale or otherwise. They may receive real estate, leaseholds, mining and boring rights and rights of way, in payment of such parts of subscriptions as they may deem advisable. Said company may erect

106 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1866 . and build,on any of theirlands,such buildings,engines,ma-
chinery and fixtures,as may be deemed convenientandproper
forcarryingon and conductingthe businessof saidcorpora-
tion. Nothing herein shallbe construed to grant banking
privileges.
§ 5. That this act shalltake effectfrom and afteritspas
sage .
Approved January16,1866.

## CHAPTER 134.

AN ACT to incorporate the Maysville Woolen Manufacturing Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That Henry Smoot, David Clark, George L. Forman, Wm. W. Baldwin, Elijah Loyd, William E. Smoot, Peter Lashbrooke, and Robert A. Cochran, their associates and successors, be, and they are hereby, constituted a body-politic and corporate, by the name and style of the Maysville Woolen Manufacturing Company; and by that name have perpetual succession, and contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in all courts in this Commonwealth and elsewhere; and are hereby established and incorporated for the purposes of manufacturing woolen goods and goods mixed with woolen and other materials, carding wool, spinning wool, cotton, or other materials used in manufacturing woolen or mixed fabrics; to have all the rights necessary to carry on said business, and all the rights granted to other corporate bodies in this Commonwealth; and may use a common seal.

§ 2. The business of the company shall be conducted under the control and management of three directors elected by the stockholders from among their number, who shall hold their office for one year and until their successors are elected and qualified. The directors shall elect one of their number president annually, and shall have power to make all by-laws, rules and regulations, not inconsistent with Constitution and laws of this Commonwealth or the Constitution and laws of the United States, necessary, or which they may deem necessary, for the regulation of themselves and for carrying on the business of said company; to fix the time of holding the annual election of directors; to fill any vacancies that may occur in the board between the annual elections, and to do all other things necessary to be done in the premises.

§ 3. The capital stock of said corporation shall be ten thousand dollars, and may be increased by the board of directors to twenty-five thousand dollars, in shares of one hundred dollars each. The board of directors shall have power to appoint any agents or officers necessary for carrying on the business, and fix their compensation.

§ 4. The said company, as a corporation, to have power to purchase and hold real estate, in fee or by lease, and to purchase, hold, and put up all necessary buildings, machinery, and other things, and to acquire and hold any and

## CHAPTER 142.
AN ACT for the benefit of Reesetta Jones.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

That Reesetta Jones be, and she is hereby, made the legal child and heiress of Rees R. Jones, of Louisville, and as such she may take by inheritance, devise, or grant.

Approved January 16, 1866.

## CHAPTER 143.
AN ACT for the relief of Richard Garnett, of Barren county.

WHEREAS, It is represented to this General Assembly that Richard Garnett, of Barren county, for many years has had and now has a lunatic negro man in the asylum at Hopkinsville, Kentucky, and has regularly been charged with and paid for the support of said negro; for remedy whereof,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That said Richard Garnett be, and he is hereby, relieved from any further payment towards the support of said lunatic in the asylum, and said negro shall be maintained during the continuance of his malady and stay in the asylum at the expense of the Commonwealth.

§ 2. This act to take effect from and after its passage.

Approved January 16, 1866.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 119

§ 3. It shall be lawful for the Montgomery and Bath county courts each, a majority of the justices of each county concurring therein, in their respective counties, by such agents as said courts may appoint, to subscribe stock in said road for and on behalf of their respective counties. The trustees of the towns of Mt. Sterling and Owingsville each, by their presiding officer, a majority concurring therein, or any other corporation in this Commonwealth, may subscribe stock in said road.

§ 4. So soon as $7,000 are subscribed to the capital stock of said company, it shall be the duty of said commissioners to give notice in the Sentinel, published in Mt. Sterling, and such other newspapers as they may select, and in such manner as they may think proper, for a meeting of the stockholders, at such time and place as they may designate, for the purpose of electing a president and three or more directors, not to exceed five; the said officers to be elected from among the stockholders, and one vote shall be allowed for each share of stock; and the said officers shall continue in office for one year and until their successors are in like manner duly elected. A majority of the board shall be competent to transact business.

§ 5. So soon as said company is organized by the election of officers, the president and directors shall possess all the powers, rights and privileges, and shall and may do all acts and things necessary for laying out and causing a survey of the most practicable route for said road, and for carrying on and completing the same, and may have and enjoy all the rights and privileges, and be subject to all the duties, qualifications, and restrictions, hereinafter provided for.

§ 6. Said president and directors shall fix and regulate the elevation and grade of said road, the width or part thereof to be covered by stone, and the width or part thereof to be elevated and graded, and left uncovered with stone; shall designate the place for the erection of gates, not exceeding one for every five miles of said road; and they may charge and receive the same tolls which are allowed by law to the Winchester and Mount Sterling turnpike road company: *Provided*, The width of said road shall not exceed forty feet, and the part covered with stone shall not be less than twelve feet; and the rate of toll shall not exceed an annual dividend of six per cent. on the capital stock required to complete said road.

§ 7. The president and directors may call on the subscribers of stock for any portion of their subscription, not exceeding one fifth of the total amount at a time, nor on shorter periods than three months, beginning with one call, until the whole shall have been paid; and if it shall be ascertained that the amount of capital before named is not sufficient to accomplish the objects of this act, the president and directors may increase the amount of capital to such sum as they shall judge sufficient to complete the road; and may, according to their direction, open books for the subscription of such additional amount of capital stock as they shall direct.

the counties of Montgomery and Bath, that may be necessary for the location of said turnpike road; and go and enter upon any lands or inclosures contiguous thereto, to examine any lands, quarries of stone, or other material necessary for the construction or use of said road; and where the use of any quarry, stone, or other material (and shall not be within two hundred yards of any person's residence) shall be necessary in the construction and for the use of said road, and the board of directors and the owner of said land, quarry, stone, or material cannot agree upon the price which shall be paid by the company for the same, application shall be made on behalf of the company to the county court of the county in which said land, quarry, stone, or other material may be situated, for a jury to assess the value of the same, and after the return and confirmation by the proper court of the inquest made by said jury, said company shall make payment of, or tender the value of any land, quarry, stone, or other material, and shall thereupon proceed to occupy, appropriate, and use the same in the construction of and for the use of the said road; and all persons employed in the construction of said road with wagons, carts, and all other necessary implements, shall have free ingress and egress to and from said lands, quarries, stone, and other materials, for the purpose aforesaid; the directors shall have power to agree with the owner of any lands, quarry, stone, and material for the same, or the use thereof.

§ 12. That when said road, or any continuous section of three and three fourths miles of said road, shall have been completed, the board of directors shall apply to the judge of the county court of Montgomery county, or any three justices of the peace of said county, whose duty it shall be to examine said section or said road, and if the same is constructed according to this charter, to certify the same, and return said certificate to the county court thereof for recordal; and on said certificate being signed by the county judge or justices aforesaid, it shall be lawful for the said president and directors to erect a toll-gate upon said section of said road, and to collect, for all travel and transit through it, tolls according to the rates hereinafter established.

§ 13. That toll-gate keepers shall be appointed in the manner, and the same rates of toll shall be fixed, and the same shall be collected in the mode prescribed in section 14 of an act approved 26th February, 1849, entitled "An act to amend the charter of the Winchester and Mt. Sterling turnpike road company," with the proviso and exemptions contained in said section of said act. And the same penalty and forfeiture shall be imposed upon and collected from every and all persons traveling upon and using said road, and willfully failing to pay tolls or avoiding the gates, that are imposed by section 15 of the last named act; and an account of receipts and expenditures are to be kept, dividends on the capital stock to be declared and paid, and tolls shall be reduced in the manner and upon the terms, and with all the provisoes, as prescribed and directed in section 16 of said last named act.

## CHAPTER 158.

AN ACT to amend the charter of Princeton College.

WHEREAS, The stockholders of Princeton College have failed to elect trustees for said college, as provided in their charter, approved February 20, 1860; and now, in order to a complete organization of the same, and the better to promote the interest of said college as an institution of learning, are desirous of electing a board of trustees for said college; therefore,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That it shall be lawful for the stockholders of Princeton College, in Caldwell county, to hold an election for trustees of said college on the second Thursday in February, 1866, in the clerk's office of the Caldwell county court, and that Jas. A. Carr, R. B. Ratcliff, and R. M. Calvert, or any two of them, are hereby appointed judges of said election; and in case either or all of them shall fail to act, the two justices of the peace living nearest the court-house in the town of Princeton shall hold said election: *Provided*, Said election shall be held in all other respects as provided in said charter: *Provided further*, If, from any cause, said election should not be held on the day above named, that it shall be lawful to hold the same any time within two months thereafter, ten days' notice having been given of the time and place of holding the same.

§ 2. Upon complying with the laws governing common schools in this State, and a majority of the trustees concurring, Princeton College shall be deemed a common school, and shall receive its quota of the common school fund.

§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved January 19, 1866.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company shall consist of two hundred thousand dollars, but may be increased by order of the corporators above named, or their successors, to any amount not exceeding one million of dollars.

§ 3. Said corporators or their successors, or a majority of them, may fill vacancies in their own body, elect such officers as they may deem necessary or proper, and by order of same fix the par value of shares, direct the manner of subscription or sale of the same, and when and in what the same shall be payable; and, until a board of directors shall be elected, they shall act as the directors of said company, with full power to manage and control its affairs.

§ 4. The number of directors and other officers, the time and manner of electing the same, and generally the manner of conducting the business of the company, shall be regulated by orders and by-laws from time to time, to be executed by said corporators or their successors.

§ 5. The said corporators or their successors may, by special order, receive real estate, either in fee or by leasehold, in payment of stock, at such price as may be agreed upon; and the stock so issued in payment therefor shall be free from all future assessments by the company, unless otherwise specially agreed.

§ 6. This act shall be in force from and after its passage.

Approved January 19, 1866.

## CHAPTER 165.

AN ACT to charter the Louisville Manufacturing Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That there shall be, and hereby is, established in the city of Louisville, a corporation by the name of “The Louisville Manufacturing Company,” with a capital stock of one hundred and twenty-five thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of one hundred dollars each, and subscribed and paid for in the manner hereinafter specified; and the said corporation, by the name aforesaid, shall be competent to contract and be contracted with, to sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, answer and defend, in all courts and places, and in all matters whatsoever, as natural persons may do, with full power to acquire, hold, use, and enjoy, and the same to sell, convey, and dispose of, all such real estate, chattels, goods and effects, as may be necessary or convenient for the transaction of its business, or which may be acquired as security for or in payment of any debt or demand; and may have and use a common seal, and alter, change, or renew the same at pleasure;

## CHAPTER 166.
1866.

AN ACT to incorporate the American Lithographic Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth Kentucky :*

§ 1. That Thomas W. Gibson, James Anderson, and Sanders D. Bruce, or a majority of them, be, and are hereby, constituted a corporation, by the name of the American Lithographic Stone Company; with power to contract and contracted with, sue and be sued, purchase and hold and sell and convey lands, in fee or by leasehold, not exceeding value five hundred thousand dollars; engage in manufacturing, mining, or boring for oil, and with all the powers necessary or proper to enable them to carry on mining and manufacturing operations.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company shall consist of five hundred thousand dollars, to be divided into such number of shares as the corporators may direct. Said corporation may organize upon such smaller amount of stock as said corporators may determine, and said stock may be increased by order of said corporators, or their successors, to any amount not exceeding one million of dollars.

§ 3. Said corporators may fill by election any vacancies in their own body; elect all such officers as they may deem necessary, and define their powers and duties; fix by order or by-laws the manner of subscribing stock, electing directors, and the number of the same, and, generally prescribe, and, from time to time, change the manner of conducting the business of said corporation.

§ 4. The corporators may, by special order, receive in payment of stock lands, either in fee or by lease, at such price as may be agreed upon, and the stock issued in payment therefor shall be free from all future assessments by said company.

§ 5. This act shall be in force from and after its passage.

Approved January 19, 1866.

**Vacancies.**

§ 6. The persons named in the second section of this act, and such as may be selected to fill a vacancy, shall hold their office until their successors are elected: *Provided*, That they shall, within six months from the passage of this act, fix the time for, and hold an election for trustees, who shall hold their office until their successors are elected and qualified. The stated annual election shall be held at the time and in the mode and manner as may be fixed by the board of trustees; all persons who may have made donations or subscription to said college to an amount equal to twenty-five dollars, and such others as the board may designate by its by-laws, may vote in person or by written proxy for trustees or other elections, and upon such questions as may be submitted to a vote. If, from any cause, the stated annual election is not held on the day fixed, another day shall be appointed, upon not less than three weeks' printed or written notices, posted at five public places in Blandville.

§ 8. The board of trustees, together with the faculty, shall have power to hold annual commencements and public examinations; and shall also have power to confer such honorary degrees and diplomas as is usual in other colleges and universities in the United States; and they shall have all the powers, privileges, and immunities, and be subject to all the pains, penalties, and liabilities of similar institutions in the State of Kentucky. Said trustees shall have full power to raise an endowment by subscription, donation, or by the receipt of legacies, and in such modes as is usual in such institutions. They may allow any denomination of Christians to endow a professorship in the literary department of said college, upon such terms as may be deemed just and reasonable; they may also unite with any benevolent, charitable, or other association or society, in the erection of the necessary buildings, upon such terms as may be agreed upon.

## CHAPTER 190.

AN ACT for the benefit of Jos. E. Mulky, late sheriff of Monroe county.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That Joseph E. Mulky, late sheriff of Monroe county, and he is hereby, relieved from the payment of three hundred and seven dollars and fifty-five cents ($307 55), uncollected militia tax for the year 1862, and the Auditor of Public Accounts is authorized to credit said Mulky's accounts by that amount.

§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved January 24, 1866.

## CHAPTER 193.

AN ACT to incorporate Malone Lodge of Accepted Masons.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That C. T. Wells, J. P. Goodson, Amos Seebolt, J. Henry Funk, and J. H. Newkirk, or such other five principal officers as may be elected from time to time, or a majority of them, be, and they are hereby, created a body corporate, under the name and style of “Malone Lodge, No. 378,” and that the officers and members of said lodge, and their successors, shall continue, and have perpetual succession, by the name and style aforesaid; they are hereby made capable in law to sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, to contract, and to answer and be answered, in all the courts of law and equity of this State or elsewhere; to make, have, and use a common seal, and the same to break, alter, or change at their pleasure.

§ 2. The said corporation shall have the right to take and hold by purchase, gift, or devise, real and personal estate not exceeding the sum of thirty thousand dollars, to dispose of and convey the same at their pleasure.

§ 3. The business and affairs of said corporation shall be under the management and control of the five principal officers, to be elected annually by the members of said lodge, and these duties shall be such as shall be prescribed by a majority thereof.

Approved January 24, 1866.
