## CHAPTER 197.

AN ACT to incorporate the Commercial Mining, Manufacturing, Refining, Exploring Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That W. H. Lape, E. W. Lape, and M. A. Lape, their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and they are hereby, constituted a body corporate and politic, by the name, style, and title of the "Commercial, Mining, Manufacturing, Refining, Exploring Company;" and by that name and style are hereby made as capable in law as natural persons to contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, to plead and be impleaded, in this Commonwealth and elsewhere, in all courts of law and equity; to have a common seal; to ordain, establish, and put in execution all such by-laws, ordinances, and regulations as shall seem necessary and convenient for the government of said corporation, not being contrary to the laws of this State or of the United States. The capital stock of said company shall be fifteen hundred thousand dollars, in shares of five dollars each, and may be increased or diminished fifty per cent. by a vote of a majority of the stockholders at any time, upon a notice of ten days; said company may, however, commence operation as soon as three thousand dollars of said capital stock shall be subscribed, and fifty per cent. thereof is paid in, and the remaining stock capital stock may be issued by the board of directors of the said company at such time as may seem to them best.

§ 2. The said company shall have power to purchase, lease, negotiate for, and hold as much real estate as they may deem necessary for the prosecution of their business, to-wit: To explore, mine for gold, silver, iron, lead, coal, and all other minerals; also to bore for salt water, petroleum, oil, or other products, and in the manufacture of the same, or either of them, on all lands now owned or which may be owned by them or either of them; to purchase, own, build flat or steamboats, roads, bridges, right of way, and all necessary buildings and machinery for the successful prosecution of said business; to hold all mining rights, and dispose of any or all of the above named materials, lands, &c., named in this instrument.

§ 3. For conducting the affairs of said company, a board of five directors shall be chosen; said board shall choose one of its number, who shall be the president of said company; and said board shall serve for one year, or until their successors are elected and qualified. The president and directors shall have power to appoint all officers, agents, and laborers under them,

§ 3. James Garnett, Jesse Murrell, Clayton Miller, Thomas
H. Frazer, S. Wheat, and T. T. Alexander, are hereby ap-
pointed commissioners, any two of whom may open books
and receive subscriptions for the capital stock; and when five
hundred shares have been subscribed, it shall be their duty to
give notice, in two or more daily newspapers published in
Louisville, Kentucky, and appoint a day for the election of a
board of directors, who shall hold their offices until the ensu-
ing annual election. The payment for the shares subscribed
shall be made as follows: Five dollars on each share at the time
of subscribing, and twenty dollars within ten days after the
election of the first board of directors, and the residue in in-
stallments of twenty-five dollars on each share every thirty
days: Provided, That after fifteen thousand dollars shall have
been paid, the board of directors shall have power to prolong
the time for the payment of the residue; and should any of
the subscribers fail to pay for their subscriptions of stock as
herein provided, after giving notice of their intention in some
daily newspaper in Louisville, Kentucky, for thirty days, the
directors may, by resolutions entered on their record, forfeit
such stock and resell the same, at such time and place as they
may deem expedient; and all partial payments made on any
stock which shall be then forfeited shall be held for the bene-
fit of the corporation. Said corporation may commence busi-
ness so soon as ten thousand dollars shall have been paid in,
and the president and directors first elected shall have made
oath to the fact of such payment before some judge or justice
of the peace.

§ 4. Said bank may receive deposits of gold, silver, bank
notes, and other notes which may be lawfully circulating as

itors, by special or general contract; may deal in the loaning of

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

## CHAPTER 205.

AN ACT to incorporate the Dane Farm Oil Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That E. L. VanWinkle, James B. Lyne, P. W. Hardin, and John M. Harlan, their associates, successors, and assigns, are hereby created a body-politic and corporate, with perpetual succession, under the corporate name of the “Dane Farm Oil Company;” and by that name may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in all courts: may have and use a common seal, may alter and change the same at pleasure; may purchase, own, and hold lands, leases, oil and other mineral grants and privileges; may sell, convey, or transfer the same to any person or persons or corporations; may have, hold, and possess any personal property, and may dispose thereof in such manner as may be deemed necessary for the successful transaction of the business of said company: may make all needful rules and regulations for the government and management of the business of the company, from time to time, as may be deemed right and proper.

§ 2. The object of said company is to develop the petroleum, rock, and carbon oils, iron, coal, and other minerals in Southern Kentucky; to that end may acquire, own, and possess, by purchase or otherwise, lands and leases, and mineral grants and privileges in that part of the State; may develop the same by boring for oil, salt water, or other liquids; may sink shafts and open mines for all manner of minerals thereon; may refine said oils and manufacture salt and all kinds of minerals, transport the same to market in the raw or manufactured state; may erect cooper-shops, machine and manufacturing-shops of all kinds and descriptions; may sink wells, establish depots, make and own landings, wharves, and boats of all descriptions; may make roads of all kinds deemed necessary for the business of said company; and shall possess all powers and privileges usually incident to mining, manufacturing, and oil companies.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall not exceed five hundred thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of not less than one hundred dollars each, to be issued in such manner as may be prescribed by the by-laws of said company; said stock may be subscribed and taken by the company in lands, leases, oil grants and wells, and in other property or money, as may be determined by the corporators and their associates; the shares of said company shall be transferable in person or by attorney upon the books of the company, which books, as well as the stock and other books of said company, shall, at all times, be open to the inspection of any stockholder; the shares of said company shall be deemed and held as personal property.

§ 4. The affairs of said company shall be conducted by a board of directors, not exceeding five in number, one of whom shall be president of the company, all of whom shall be stock-

## CHAPTER 212.
1866.

AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Newport.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That hereafter all appeals taken from judgments of the mayor of the city of Newport shall be taken to the Campbell circuit court, instead of the county or quarterly court, as now authorized by law.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved January 26, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 214.

AN ACT for the benefit of Joseph McCarroll, sheriff of Christian county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the Auditor of Public Accounts be authorized, and he is hereby directed, to issue his quietus to Joseph McCarroll, sheriff of Christian, for all uncollected militia fines in said county: *Provided*, This act shall not be so construed as to release him from the payment of the sum of one hundred dollars already collected on account of militia fines.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved January 26, 1866.

## CHAPTER 224.
AN ACT to authorize Mary A. Graves to qualify as guardian of her son, Thos. J. Wilson.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*
§ 1. That Mary A. Graves, of the county of Ballard, be, and is hereby, authorized and empowered to qualify in the proper court as guardian of her son, Thomas J. Wilson, as if she was a *feme sole*; and said Mary A. shall give bond, with good security, as now required by law, which shall be binding upon the parties executing the same as fully as if she was unmarried.

## CHAPTER 227.
AN ACT for the benefit of J. W. Cardwell, late sheriff of Breathitt county.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*
§ 1. That John W. Cardwell, collector of the revenue of Breathitt county by virtue of an act approved 3d June, 1865, and by order of the Breathitt county court, be allowed to return a delinquent list, under the same rules and regulations sheriffs may by law, and that he have until the 1st day of June, 1866, to make out and return said list.
§ 2. That this act shall be in force from its passage.
Approved January 26, 1866.

## CHAPTER 228.
AN ACT for the benefit of the executors of R. R. Revill, deceased.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*
§ 1. That the further time of two years, from and after the 1st day of March, 1866, be, and the same is hereby, given to L. B. Gale and E. J. Revill, executors of R. R. Revill, deceased, within which to issue and collect the fee bills of said Revill as late clerk of the Court of Appeals and of the Owen county court, during which period said fee bills shall have the same distrainable force as clerks' fee bills now have by law: *Provided*, Said executors shall be liable under existing laws for the issual and collection of illegal fee bills.
§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.
Approved January 26, 1866.

upon the same, or upon the public streets, suitable shade trees; and any person who shall injure or destroy any shade tree or shrubbery upon the public grounds, or upon the public streets, whether planted by authority of the trustees or by private citizens, shall be subject to a fine of twenty-five dollars, to be recovered by warrant before the police judge, and enforced as other fines are enforced. They shall have power to require the owner of any lot to make a brick or stone sidewalk in front thereof, not less than four feet wide, and at such grade as they shall direct; and if, after a written notice of not less than three months, served upon such owner, he or she shall fail to have said sidewalk made in pursuance of said notice, the said trustees shall cause the same to be done at the expense of the owner, and shall have a lien upon said lot for the amount of said expense, which may be enforced by a suit in the Mason circuit court, in the name of the trustees, and at the cost of the owner; and where several owners of lots are in default, they may be joined as defendants in one suit.

1866.

§ 16. The board of trustees shall have power to appoint a treasurer, who shall be a resident of the town, and a qualified voter, who, before entering upon the duties of his office, shall take an oath faithfully to perform the same, and also the oath required of other officers by the constitution and laws of the State; and shall give bond for such an amount and with such securities as the board of trustees shall approve, conditioned to pay over and account for all moneys which shall come into his hands while in office, and for the prompt and faithful discharge of all duties imposed upon him as treasurer of the town. It shall be his duty to receive all moneys of the town derived from taxes, licenses, wharfage, fines, or other sources; but shall not pay out or expend the same in anywise except upon the order of the board of trustees, signed by the president, which order shall specify for what purpose the same is to be paid. He shall keep a careful and correct account of all receipts and expenditures of the trustees, and a separate account of the twenty-five per cent. of the *ad valorem* taxes set apart for the payment of the railroad debt, and of the payments made out of said fund, which accounts shall always be open for the inspection of the trustees or their proper committee. He shall make reports of the condition of the treasury from time to time, as the trustees may require, and at the regular meeting of the trustees in March of each year shall report to the trustees a full statement of the receipts and expenditures for the year. He shall receive for his services such compensation or salary as may be provided by ordinance; and may be removed at any time, upon proper notice, for good cause shown. If for any cause the office of treasurer shall become vacant, the trustees may appoint another.

Treasurer—his powers and duties.

§ 17. The police judge of East Maysville shall have power to commit all offenders liable to commitment to the county jail of Mason county, and the jailer thereof shall receive and keep the same as other prisoners are received and kept.

Offenders may be committed to county jail.

CHAPTER 242 .
AN ACT to incorporatethe Beargrassrailwaycompany.
Be itenactedbytheGeneralAssemblyofthe Commonwealthof
Kentucky :
§ 1. That Joshua B. Bowles,Thomas S. Kenneday ,Willian
C. Williams , Joshua B. Parks , J. A. Moore ,Richard Isaacs
George H. Carey,James Harrison,andFrederickG. Edwards
and theirsuccessors,be,and they arehereby,createdabody
corporateand politic,by thename of the"Beargrassrailway
company ," forthe term of thirtyyears, with allthe power
and authorityincidenttocorporations,forthepurposeshereinafter mentioned .
§ 2. The corporationis hereby authorizedand empowere
to contract,maintain,and operatea singleor doubletrac

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 199

railway, with all necessary and convenient tracks for turnouts, side tracks, and appendages, from Beargrass creek, within the city of Louisville, or outside of the limits of said city, along the line or near thereto of the Shelbyville and Louisville turnpike road to Gilman, or near thereto, at the intersection of the said road and the Westport road; and said corporation may agree with said Shelbyville and Louisville turnpike road company to locate said railway in, on, over, or along said turnpike road, or may locate said railway along or near to the line of said turnpike road; and for that purpose may, by contract, obtain the written consent of the owners of land over which said railway may be located for the right of way for the same, or may proceed by writ of *ad quod damnum* to condemn said land for said railway and turnouts, side tracks, and for car-sheds, stables, and appendages, in the same way and with the same responsibilities as prescribed by the charter of the Louisville and Nashville railroad company; and may locate said railway so as to cross said turnpike road, or the Louisville and Frankfort railroad track, but not so as to inconvenience the use of said turnpike road or railroad, or to obstruct the same.

1866.

§ 3. The said Beargrass railway company shall not be liable for any baggage carried on said railway, kept in and under the care of its owner, his servant, or agent; and the capital stock of said company shall be twenty-five thousand dollars, and may be increased to one hundred thousand dollars or less sum, as may be agreed on by the stockholders of said corporation at a general meeting. The stock shall be divided into shares of one hundred dollars each, and issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions as the board of directors of said corporation may direct.

§ 4. The affairs of said corporation shall be managed by seven directors, one of whom shall be president, all of whom shall be stockholders in said corporation; the first board of directors shall consist of Joshua B. Bowles, W. C. Williams, Thomas S. Kenneday, Richard Isaacs, J. A. Moore, George H. Carey, and James Harrison, who shall continue in office until their successors shall be elected by a majority in interest of the stockholders of said corporation; and the board of directors chosen by the stockholders shall continue in office for one year, or until their successors are elected and qualified. If any of the above named directors shall decline or refuse to act, a majority of the others shall fill the vacancy by appointing or choosing some one else; they may adopt such by-laws, rules and regulations, for the government of said corporation, and the management of its affairs and business, as they may deem proper, not inconsistent with the laws of this State and United States; the said corporators, or any of them, may open books of subscription, and receive subscriptions to the capital stock of said corporation, and such books of subscription may be opened, and subscriptions received, at such times and places, and upon such notice thereof, as three of the corporators may deem right and proper.

## CHAPTER 248.

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the town of St. Mary's, in Marion county."

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the election of the police judge and town marshal of St. Mary's, in Marion county, be declared legal as though it had taken place on the day indicated by law.

§ 2. That on the first Saturday in March, 1866, the qualified voters of the town of St. Mary's shall elect five trustees; the election of said trustees shall be held under the supervision of officers appointed by the police judge of said town.

§ 3. The police judge shall have all the powers conferred by law upon justices of the peace in this Commonwealth, and all fines imposed by the trustees of St. Mary's shall be recoverable before the police judge of the town of St. Mary's.

§ 4. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved January 27, 1866.

---

232 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1866 . CHAPTER 284 .
AN ACT for the benefitof the Hustonville, Liberty,and Columbia turnpike
road company .
WHEREAS ,It has been representedthat a largepartofthe
bauling done on the Hustonville, Liberty, and Columbia
turnpike is on that part of the road between the toll-gates,
and for which no tollis or can be collected; for remedy
whereor ,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyofthe Commonwealthof
Kentucky :
§ 1. That an act,entitled"An act to incorporatethe Hus-
tonville, Liberty, and Columbia turnpike road company,"
approved February 6, 1864, be, and the same is hereby,so
amended as to authorize the president and directorsof said
turnpike road company to collectfrom any person or persons
who may use said road for the purpose of hauling,foreach
mile of the road used,at the same rate now charged by said
company forthehaulingwhich passesthrough thetollgates
on said road: Provided, That nothing in thisact shallbe so
construed to allow tollto be charged any person who uses
said road in hauling from one part of his or her farm to
another .
§2 That allthestockholders,with theirhands belongingto
saidcompany ,livingwithin one half mile of said road,shall
hereafterbe subjectto work on the repairsof said road,and
be exempt from allotherroads.
§ 3. That the presidentof said company shallhave power
to callupon said stockholders,at any time he thinksproper
to do so,towork on the repairsof saidroad,firstgivingthem
at leastthreedays'notice: Provided, however, That he shall
not requirethe hands to work on said road more than four
days in one year .
§ 4. If any stockholderin said company , livingwithinone
half mile of saidroad,when notifiedby the presidentof said
road,shallfailor refuseto attendwith the hands belonging
to them ,prepared with suitable tools to work said road ,they
shallbe liableto the same penaltiesthat is now allowed by
law for failureto work on county roads,and recoverablein
thesame way; and any finesrecoveredshallbe appliedtothe
use of said road .
§ 5. This actto take effectfrom itspassage.
Approved February2,1866.

mon school laws to be educated,shallbe deemed a qualified

voter under this act: Provided . That when a vote for such a
tax shallbe taken ,votes shall be received from nine o'clock
A. M. tillfiveo'clockP. M .: Providedfurther, That previously
to the taking of such a vote,notices,signedby the common
schoolcommissioner of the county in which such districtlies,

§ 2. That the amount thus paid out of the treasury shall be refunded thereto out of whatever, if any, excess of surplus, over and above what may, by any other special act passed and approved during the present session of the Legislature, be otherwise appropriated; and if said excess of surplus shall not be sufficient for the purpose of refunding to the treasury the said amount, then whatever remains unrefunded shall be deducted from the amount named to the credit of said county in the bond issued for surplus on the 1st day of July, 1865, at the time of the cancellation and reissual of the said bond on the 1st day of July, 1866.

## CHAPTER 299.

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled “An act for the benefit of H. L. Anderson, of Graves county,” approved February 23, 1865.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the act, entitled “An act for the benefit of H. L. Anderson, of Graves county,” approved February 23, 1865, is hereby amended so as to vacate the entry made by him and the patent which issued thereon of the lands described in the preamble to said act.

§ 2. That that part of said act which authorized the Auditor to draw his warrant in favor of the said H. L. Anderson for the sum of fourteen dollars eighty-seven and one half cents, is hereby repealed, and the receiver at Mayfield is directed to refund to the said Anderson the said sum of fourteen dollars eighty-seven and one half cents, out of any money now in or which may hereafter come into his hands, from the sale of public lands lying west of the Tennessee river, this being the full amount of the entry price paid into the receiver’s office by him.

§ 3. This amendment to take effect from its passage.

Approved February 2, 1866.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
## CHAPTER 306.
AN ACT for the benefit of the town of Big Spring.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That hereafter the prudential, fiscal, and municipal concerns of Big Spring shall be vested in five trustees, who shall be elected annually on the first Monday in April, by the voters who are duly qualified as such under the Constitution, and by all white male citizens of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, who are freeholders in said town; said trustees, when elected, shall hold their offices for the term of one year, and until their successors are elected and duly qualified. That said trustees, before they enter upon the duties of their office, shall take an oath before some justice of the peace or police or other judge, they they will faithfully, and without partiality or affection to any one, discharge the duties of trustees to said town during their continuance in office.

§ 2. That said trustees, after their qualification, shall proceed to elect one of their number chairman, who shall preside at their meetings, and have power to convene the board, when, in his opinion, the interest of the town demands it. It shall be his duty to see that all the ordinances and by-laws of said town are duly executed.

§ 3. That said trustees and their successors in office shall be a body-politic and corporate, and shall be known by the name and style of "The Trustees of Big Spring;" and by that name shall be capable in law of contracting and being contracted with, suing and being sued, pleading and being impleaded, answering and being answered, and of defending and being defended, in all courts and places; and do all acts, matters and things which a body-politic or corporate, having perpetual succession, can lawfully and rightfully do.

§ 4. That said trustees, or a majority of them, shall have power to make or receive all necessary conveyances in relation to said town; they shall have power over the streets, alleys, and sidewalks town, or which may hereafter be opened, and may direct the improvements of the same as they may deem most beneficial to the interest of said town; they shall have power to levy and collect an *ad valorem* tax on the property in said town not exceeding twenty-five cents on the one hundred dollars in any one year, and a poll-tax on each white male over the age of twenty-one years not exceeding two dollars; they shall have power to tax auction sales, shows, and exhibitions for money or profit, such sum as they may deem proper and just, or as may be provided in their by-laws; they shall have the power to suppress all tippling and gambling houses, and to fine all those who may violate their by-laws any sum not exceeding twenty dollars for each offense, except for disturbance of religious worship, riots, routs, unlawful assemblies, breaches of the peace, tippling-houses, and gambling-houses, where the penalty may be one hundred dollars, which may be recovered before the police judge hereinafter provided for, or any justice of the peace; they shall have power to declare what are nuisances, and to remove the same; they

§ 6. That the said companies may call on the cities of Louisville, Covington, and Newport, or on any one or more of the counties through which the said branch road may be located, or on any county adjacent thereto, to purchase of the bonds herein authorized to be issued, to an amount not exceeding two hundred and fifty thousand dollars each from the cities of Louisville, Covington, and Newport, and not exceeding one hundred and fifty thousand dollars from any one of the said counties; and upon such call or application, it shall be the duty of the mayor of the respective cities, and of the county judge of such counties respectively so called on, to give public notice, designating a day and causing polls to be opened at all the election precincts in their respective cities or counties, and cause a vote to be taken by the qualified voters for and against such call or application, of which notice shall be given within ten days after said application is made; and the time and places for such voting shall be published in the manner now required by law in the election of members for the State Legislature, for at least three weeks preceding the day of such voting; and if at such election or voting a majority of the votes cast shall be in favor of such purchase, then it shall be the duty of the mayors and county judges of the respective cities and counties to make such purchase for their respective city or county in the amount specified in the call; and at any such voting or election, it shall be the duty of the officers now so required by law to appoint judges and clerks, and other officers, to superintend and conduct said votings or elections in the counties, with such powers and under such regulations as are prescribed and given to them at State or county elections for members of the Legislature, and in the cities with powers and under such regulations as are prescribed and given at city elections; and to make their returns of such votings or elections as in other county, city, or State elections. The county judges and the mayors of cities shall examine and compare the polls of the vote so taken in their respective counties and cities, and declare and certify the result thereof, which, if in favor of such purchase, shall be recorded in the county or city clerk's offices of the respective counties and cities.

## CHAPTER 312.

**AN ACT** to legalize the proceedings of the Graves county court.

WHEREAS, The presiding judge of the Graves county court has failed to sign the minutes and orders of said court, from the July term up to and including the December term thereof, 1865; therefore,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That all the orders and judgments of said court made as aforesaid, be, and they are hereby, ratified and confirmed, and made valid, and all the legal acts of the officers, executors, and administrators, &c., made and done under and by virtue of said orders, are hereby legalized.

§ 2. That this act to take effect from its passage.

Approved February 2, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 313.

**AN ACT** creating the office of county treasurer for Floyd county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That there is hereby created the office of treasurer of Floyd county, whose duties shall be to settle with and adjust the claims of the county of Floyd against the county judge thereof, and to this end he may institute in his own name, for the use of said county, all necessary proceedings against said judge.

§ 2. The said treasurer shall be elected by the justices of the peace, a majority thereof being present; but no one of said justices shall be deemed eligible to said office.

§ 3. Before said treasurer enter on the discharge of his duties, he shall execute a bond payable to said county court, with good security, to be approved by the said justices, for the faithful performance of his duties, and that he pay over, according to law, all sums which he may collect.

§ 4. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved February 2, 1866.

WHEREAS ,G. Mansfield,G. T. Blakey ,and H. C. Gillium,
on behalfand under theauthorityof the Logan Presbytery
of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church , did,on the 27th
November ,1865,make saleof theCumberland Presbyterian
Church property in Russellville,Kentucky ,and the congrega-
tionand otherpartiesinteresteddesirethat saidsalemay be
made valid; therefore,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyofthe Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That saidsalebe,andthesame ishereby,legalized,and
the saidG. Mansfield, G. T. Blakey, and H. C. Gillium, or
any two of them ,or, in the eventthey do not or cannot, from
anycause,act,any committeeappointedby saidPresbytery,be,
and theyarehereby, authorizedtomake the necessarydeed
and billsof saleto the purchasersaccording to the terms and
conditions of the sale.
§ 2. If,from any cause,the conditionsof sale are not com-
plied with,rendering a resale necessary,the saidparties,or
any othercommittee appointed by the saidPresbytery, shal
have power to sellsaidproperty,or any part thereof,and make
proper conveyances to the purchaser or purchasers.
§ 3. This actshallbe in forcefrom itspassage.
Approved February 2, 1866 .

§ 1. That D. R. Alexander,S. H. Southwick , D. B. Dewey

porate powers .

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

same to hold, use, and enjoy, any real or personal property, bonds, notes, or obligations for money, which the board of directors may deem advisable for the success of the business of the company, and the same, or any amount or part thereof, to sell, convey, lease, assign, transfer, or otherwise dispose of; and generally to do and perform, for the good of said company, whatsoever shall lawfully pertain to such corporate bodies; and may have and use a corporate seal, and alter, break, and renew the same at the pleasure of the directors.

The above named corporators, or their assigns, shall have power to organize said company, and shall constitute the first board of directors, and shall hold their office until others are duly elected and qualified, and may appoint such officers and agents as they may deem necessary; a majority of said board shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company shall consist of not less than $50,000 nor more than $200,000 at the organization thereof, but by order of the board of directors may be increased to the amount of $500,000; said stock shall be divided into shares of $100 each, on which not less than five per cent. shall be paid up in cash at time of organization, the remainder to be secured in such manner and at such times as the corporators or board of directors may determine.

Capital stock.

§ 3. The affairs of said company shall be managed by a board of directors, to consist of not less than five nor more than twenty stockholders, as may be regulated by the by-laws made to govern said company; and said directors shall be chosen by ballot from among the corporators or stockholders; and a majority of the board shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business until the board consist of more than five directors, after which the president and three directors shall constitute a quorum.

Who to manage.

§ 4. An annual election for directors shall be held at the office of the company at such time as the board may appoint; and said directors shall hold their offices for one year or until their successors are duly elected and qualified; vacancies occurring in the board may be filled at any meeting of the directors.

Annual election.

§ 5. In all elections of officers or directors by stockholders, each share, to the number of five (5), shall be entitled to a vote, and every ten (10) shares in addition shall entitle the holder to one additional vote; but no person shall be entitled to a vote who is not a corporator or bona fide stockholder; and shares may be voted upon by the executor or administrator of the deceased owner or by proxy.

How stock voted.

§ 6. The board of directors shall elect a president, vice president, secretary, treasurer, and general agent, who shall hold their offices for one year or until others are chosen, who shall have power to appoint such subordinate officers and agents, and at such places as they shall deem proper, for the transacting of business of said company, and to allow such compensation as may, from time to time, be agreed upon, and be required to take bond and security for the faithful discharge of their respective duties and trusts; and said board of directors

§ 2. Said bank shall be under the direction and control of seven directors, each of whom shall be stockholders; and, after the first election, shall be elected every two years, commencing on the first Monday of January after the first election, and on the first Monday of January every two years thereafter, and hold office until their successors are elected and qualified; they shall elect one of their number president, and shall hold regular meetings at such times as may be fixed by the by-laws; and other meetings may be held whenever deemed necessary; and in case of the death or resignation of any director, the vacancy shall be filled by the board of directors. The directors shall have power to receive subscriptions for so many of the shares of the stock of said company remaining unsold as they may at any time choose to sell; to declare dividends of the profits arising out of the business, and to appoint such officers, agents, and servants as they may deem necessary to conduct the business of the bank, and pay them such sums as wages and salaries, and take from them such bond to secure the faithful performance of their duties, as they shall think proper and reasonable; the stock shall be deemed personal property, and shall be assignable according to such rules as the board of directors shall from time to time establish; but said corporation shall have a lien on the stock to secure any indebtedness by the stockholder.

Kentucky :

saidtown may ,by a petitionto the county court,have any
streetor alleywithin the corporatelimitsthereofextended,
or open new streetsand alleys.

moned or notifiedby the sheriffor other officer,at leastten

## CHAPTER 407.

AN ACT to amend the charter of the Georgetown and Long Lick turnpike road company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the act of assembly, entitled "An act to incorporate the Georgetown and Long Lick turnpike road company," approved February 16th, 1858, be so amended as to permit said company to extend their road from its present terminus, at Asa Owens', down Long Lick branch to the bridge across Lytle's Fork, near the mill of J. J. Browing, and for that purpose said company are authorized to increase their capital stock to twenty-five thousand dollars, and George W. Burch, Asa Owens, Thomas J. Nutter, Trusten Woolen, Joseph B. Beatty, James D. Smith, John G. Morrison, and M. Polk, or some two or more of them, who are appointed commissioners to procure subscriptions of stock to said company; and they shall procure a book, and the subscribers of stock to said company shall enter into the following obligation. "We, whose names are hereunto subscribed, promise to pay to the president, directors, and company of the Georgetown and Long Lick turnpike road company, the sum of one hundred dollars for each share of stock taken by us in said company, agreeable to an act of the General Assembly incorporating said company and the act amendatory thereto. Witness our hands this, the ______ day of ______."

§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved February 6, 1866.

## CHAPTER 428.

AN ACT for the benefit of the administrator of W. B. Wall, deceased.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the personal representative of William B. Wall, deceased, late clerk of the county court and circuit court of Daviess county, be authorized to list with the proper collecting officers of this Commonwealth all fees of said offices due to the said Wall at the time of his death, and which remained uncollected at the time of his death, and that the officers with whom said fee bills, or any of them, may be listed by said personal representative for collection, shall have the further time of two years in which to distrain for and collect same.

§ 2. That before any officer in whose hands said fee bills may be placed for collection as aforesaid shall be authorized to distrain for said demands, or any of them, the personal representative of said Wall shall, in the county court of Daviess county, give bond, with good personal security, to be answerable according to the laws now in force to any person or persons who may be injured by the issuing of any illegal fee bill due said Wall, or for any illegal distress or other improper conduct in the collection of said fee bills.

§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 7, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 429.

AN ACT for the benefit of the officers and soldiers of the 31st Regiment of the Enrolled Militia.

WHEREAS, The Governor of this Commonwealth, at the request of the commanding officer of the United States forces in Eastern Kentucky, did, in the month of May, 1864 (the emergency requiring it), call into active service a detachment of one hundred men of the 31st Regiment of Enrolled Militia in Carter county; that said detachment so called out served forty-three days and have not been paid; therefore,

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the Governor of this Commonwealth be authorized to pay, or cause to be paid, out of the military fund, to each soldier and officer of said detachment, whatever may be

350 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1866 . found due to them forsaidserviceupon a correctadjustment
of theiraccounts; but beforesuch payment be made ,a mus-
terrollshallbe made out by the commanding officer,contain-
ing the length of time that each officerand privateactually
served, accompanied by the affidavitsof the Colonel and
Major of said31stRegiment .
§ 2. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved February7, 1866.

## CHAPTER 432.

AN ACT to incorporate the Tuscarora Oil Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That E. L. VanWinkle, John S. VanWinkle, and Jas. B. Lyne, their associates, successors, and assigns, are hereby created a body-politic and corporate, with perpetual succession, under the corporate name of the "Tuscarora Oil Company;" and by that name may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in all courts; may have and use a common seal, may alter and change the same at pleasure; may purchase, own, and hold lands, leases, oil and other mineral grants and privileges, to an extent necessary for the business and object of this corporation: *Provided*, That the company shall at no time hold lands exceeding one thousand acres in extent; may sell, convey, or transfer the same to any person or persons or corporations; may have, hold, and possess any personal property, and may dispose thereof in such manner as may be deemed necessary for the successful transaction of the business of said company; may make all needful rules and regulations for the government and management of the business of the company, from time to time, as may be deemed right and proper.

§ 2. The object of said company is to develop the petroleum, rock, and carbon oils, iron, coal, and other minerals in Southern Kentucky; to that end may acquire, own, and possess, by purchase or otherwise, lands and leases, and mineral grants and privileges in that part of the State; may develop the same by boring for oil, salt water, or other liquids; may sink shafts, open mines for all manner of minerals thereon; may refine said oils, manufacture salt and all kinds of minerals, transport the same to market in the raw or manufactured state; may erect cooper-shops, machine and manufacturing-shops of all kinds and descriptions; may sink wells, establish depots, make and own landings, wharves, and boats of all descriptions; may make roads of any kind which may be

## CHAPTER 444.

AN ACT to incorporate the Security Insurance Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky :*

§ 1. That C. C. Spencer, J. Fry Lawrence, George W. McCarn, W. H. Didrick, John B. Cleveland, and all other persons who may hereafter become associated with them in the manner herein prescribed, shall be, and are hereby, constituted a body-corporate and politic, by the name and style of the Security Insurance Company; and by that name may sue and be sued, appear, prosecute, and defend, in any court of record or other court or place whatsoever; and may have and use a common seal, and alter and renew the same at pleasure; may purchase and hold such personal and real estate as may be necessary to effect the object of their association, and may sell and convey the same at pleasure: *Provided*, Such real estate shall not exceed in value at any one time the sum of fifty thousand dollars, except such as may be taken for debts due the company, or as part of the guarantee capital; they may establish and put in execution such by-laws, ordinances, and resolutions, not contrary to the laws of this State or of the United States, as may be necessary or convenient for their regulation and government, and for the management of their affairs.

CHAPTER 446 .
AN ACT for the benefit of James Lindon , sheriffof Breathitt county.
WHEREAS , James Lindon , sheriffof Breathittcounty,has
recentlybeen electedto the officeof sheriff; and whereas,he
isrequiredto collectthe revenue of saidcounty,fortheyears
1862, 1863, 1864,and 1865; and whereas, itisimpossiblefor
him to collectsaidrevenue in thetime allowedby law ; there-
fore,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That James Lindon ,sheriffof Breathittcounty,be al-

to return his delinquentlist: Provided,That the suretiesof
saidsheriffshallpersonallyappear beforethe county courtof
saidcounty,and give theirassentto saidextensionbeforethe
sheriff shall have the benefit of this act .
§ 2. That this act shall be in force from itspassage .

by that name shallbe competent to sue and be sued,in the
courtsof this Commonwealth ; may have and use a common
seal,and change the same at pleasure; may establishand alter
by -laws for the regulationof said company ; may purchase,
hold, and disposeof real estate,not exceeding in value one
hundred thousanddollars,leases,mines,mineral,and personal
property; may mine , smelt, and vend mineral products of
every kind ; and in general may prosecutethe business of
mining,smelting,and dealinginmineralsubstances.§ 2. The capitalstockof said company shallbe two hun-
dred and fiftythousand dollars, divided into shares of fifty
dollars each .
§ 3. The corporatorshereinbeforenamed ,or any four of
them , shallhave power to organize saidcompany , and upon
the organizationof the company ,a meeting of stockholders

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

shall be called, which meeting shall choose five directors to manage the business of the company.

§ 4. The board of directors shall continue in office for one year, or until their successors are elected by the stockholders.

§ 5. The board of directors shall choose a president, secretary, and treasurer, who shall hold their offices for the term of one year, and until their successors are duly chosen.

§ 6. There shall be an annual meeting of the stockholders for the election of directors of the company, and the said company shall have and keep an office and agent in this State.

§ 7. Nothing in this act shall be construed to confer banking privileges on said company.

§ 8. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved February 8, 1866.

## CHAPTER 450.

AN ACT to incorporate the Augusta Coal and Fuel Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That Thornton F. Marshall, Abraham Baker, Sylvanus W. McGibben, Vachel Weldin, James B. Ryan, William J. Rankins, and Joseph Doniphan, and their associates and successors, be, and they are hereby, created a body-politic and corporate, by the name and style of the “Augusta Coal and Fuel Company” (to be located at Augusta in this State); and by that name shall have perpetual succession; may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in all courts and places; have a common seal, and alter and break the same at pleasure; may make all necessary by-laws for the transaction of the business and government of said company, not inconsistent with the constitution and laws of this State or the United States.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company shall not exceed twenty-five thousand dollars, which shall be divided into shares of fifty dollars each, to be subscribed and paid for in such manner as said company may, by its by-laws, prescribe.

§ 3. That said company shall have power to appoint any one or more of its members or other persons to manage, contract, and direct the business of said company according to the by-laws, rules and regulations, which said company may adopt.

§ 4. That said company may acquire and hold in its corporate name such real estate, not exceeding in value the capital stock, and personal estate, by contract, as they may deem necessary or proper for the carrying on their business, and also the mining and transporting of coal to their place of business, together with such quantities of land as may be necessary for the establishment of landings and depots for the same.

§ 5. That said company, when fully organized, shall cause a book to be opened and kept subject to the inspection of any member of said company, which shall contain the names of

380 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1866 . allthe members of said company ,and the number of shares
owned by each; and the said shares may be transferredon
saidbook in the manner tobe prescribedby theby-lawsof
saidcompany .
§ 6. The principalbusinessofficeof saidcompany shallbe
inAugusta,Kentucky; and nothingin thischartershallbe
construedintoconferringbanking powers upon thiscompany
but to transactthe business of mining , transporting, and
sellingcoal,under such rulesand regulationsas the company
may directby theirby -laws. This chartershallbe forthirty
years .
§ 7. The Legislaturereservesthe rightto alter, amend,on
repealthischarterat pleasure.
§ 8. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved February8,1866.

## CHAPTER 466.

AN ACT to incorporate the Russell Creek and Green River Oil Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky* :

§ 1. That E. H. Hobson, L. Hartfield, their associates, successors, and assigns, be, and they are hereby, created a body corporate and politic, by the name and style of the "Russell Creek and Green River Oil Company," and by that name and style shall have perpetual succession, with power to contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, in that name and style, in all courts and places; to have a common seal, which may be altered or broken at pleasure; to engage in mining and boring for petroleum, salt water, and other volatile and mineral substances, and in the manufacturing, refining, transporting, and selling oil, salt, and other products of said business, and of the lands now owned, or which may hereafter be acquired by them in this State; to hold their meetings, and transport and sell their oil in the crude or manufactured condition, and other minerals, within or without this Commonwealth; and to have all other powers needful and proper for the successful prosecution of their business, and for the execution of the powers herein granted.

§ 2. That the corporators may organize said company by the appointment of a president, and such other officers or managers as they may deem necessary, at such time and place as they may designate by notice previously given; and when thus organized, the said company shall have power to make such by-laws, rules and regulations, as they may deem necessary, from time to time, for the government and prosecution of their business, not inconsistent with the laws of this Commonwealth or of the United States.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall be one hundred

## CHAPTER 471.
1866.

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act for the benefit of Clinton county."
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§1. That section 1st, chapter 221, entitled "An act for the benefit of Clinton county," approved February 3d, 1837, be, and the same is amended, as to appropriate the money obtained for the sale of vacant land in said county to the use of public buildings, in place of the improvements of the roads in said county as provided in said act.

§2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved February 9, 1866.

## CHAPTER 472.

AN ACT to incorporate the Bowling Green Building Company.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§1. That James A. Graham, William Cook, T. C. Calvert, Joseph Lilianthal, P. J. Potter, S. A. Barclay, John C. Underwood, and their associates, who have subscribed stock for the purpose of purchasing grounds and erecting a town hall and other buildings at Bowling Green, Warren county, Kentucky, whenever the sum of twenty thousand dollars is subscribed, and who contemplate enlarging their stock, shall be a body-politic and corporate, to be styled the "Bowling Green Building Company," with power of contracting and being contracted with, of suing and being sued; to have and use a common seal, and renew and alter the same at pleasure; to purchase, receive conveyances of, hold, sell, and convey all such real and personal estate as may be necessary and requisite in the management and conducting of the business of said company, and to have all the usual powers incident to corporations of like character.

§2. The capital stock in said company shall not be less than twenty thousand dollars, nor more than one hundred thousand dollars, divided into shares of one hundred dollars each; and if two hundred shares or more have been already or may be hereafter subscribed, the subscribers may proceed to organize by electing directors, five days' notice having been first given by any two of the stockholders.

§3. The prudential and fiscal affairs of said company and the management and control of its property, shall be vested in five directors, elected annually by the stockholders on such day of the year, after the first election, as said stockholders may designate, each share of stock giving to the stockholder one vote, which may be cast in person or by proxy; the directors shall elect a president from among their number, at their first meeting after their election, three members constituting a quorum; and the president and directors shall have power to fill any vacancies in offices until the next regular election.

§4. The stock shall be payable at such times and in such

## CHAPTER 473.
1866.

AN ACT to incorporate the United Evangelic Christian School Society of Louisville.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§1. That Henry Wehmhoff, Fr. W. Johanboke, Henry Block, Henry Waterman, Samuel Sulzer, Geo. H. Kopmier, J. H. Richterkessing, Fred. Wedenkemper, and Philip Tomppert, jr., their associates and successors, to be incorporated by the name of "The United Evangelic Christian School Society;" and shall have, by that name, perpetual succession; use a common seal, contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, and acquire, hold, and dispose of property, real and personal; but the property held at any one time shall not exceed twenty-five thousand dollars.

§2. The capital stock of the society shall be five thousand dollars, divided into shares of fifty dollars each, and may be increased, from time to time, but not to exceed the stipulated sum of twenty-five thousand dollars.

§3. The affairs of this society shall be managed from the time of the passage of this act until the first Monday of August, 1867, by the corporators of this charter, or until their successors are elected and qualified; the first election may be fixed by the present corporators within thirty days from the passage of this act, and the officers shall be chosen from among their body.

§4. The regular annual election shall be held on the first Monday of each year, and shall be conducted according to the constitution and by-laws of this society.

§5. The present corporators shall have the power to frame a constitution, and make such by-laws as may be considered to be to the best interest of the society, and for their government; and the same may be so altered and amended, from time to time, according to the constitution of this society; nothing, however, to be in conflict with the laws of the United States, this State, or the laws of the city of Louisville.

§6. This corporation is not to engage in any trade or business except the education of youth.

§7. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 9, 1866.

## CHAPTER 474.

AN ACT to legalize the orders of the Ohio county and quarterly courts, made by John Stevens, a justice of the peace.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

That the acts of John Stevens, justice of the peace of Ohio county, when acting as presiding judge of said county, be, and are hereby, declared legal.

Approved February 9, 1866.

§ 17. That whenever it shall be necessary for said company to have, use, or occupy any lands, materials, or other property, in order to the construction or repair of any part of said road, or its works or necessary buildings, the president and directors of said company, or their agents, or those contracting with them for working or repairing the same, may immediately take and use the same, they having first caused the property wanted to be viewed by a jury (formed in the manner hereinbefore prescribed in those cases where the property is to be changed or altered by admixture with other substances before such alteration is made); and it shall not be necessary, after such view, in order to the use and occupation of the same, to note the issue of the proceedings upon such view; and the inquest of the jury, after the payment or tender of such valuation, shall be a bar for taking and using such property, whether begun before or after such confirmation, or the payment of such valuation.

Proceedings when necessary for company to take land, &c., for repair of road.

§ 18. That the president and directors shall have power to purchase with the funds of said company, and place on any railroads constructed by them under this act, all rolling stock and every thing necessary which they may deem proper for the purposes of transportation on said road; and they shall have the power to charge for tolls and the transportation of merchandise and property, of any kind whatever, transported along said railway, any sum not exceeding the rates charged by the Louisville and Nashville railroad company; that it shall not be lawful for any other company or person to travel upon or use any of the roads of said company, or to transport persons or property thereon without license and permission of the president and directors thereof; and the said road or roads, with all their improvements or profits, and all the carriages, vehicles, and machinery.

May provide rolling stock, &c.

Not to charge more than Louisville and Nashville railroad for transportation.

§ 19. That the said president and directors shall annually or semi-annually declare and make such dividend as they may deem proper of the nett profits arising from the resources of said company, after deducting the necessary probable and contingent expenses, and that they shall divide the same among the stockholders of said company in proportion to their respective shares.

Dividends.

tucky, to serve as directors of said institution until the first Monday in July following, and until their successors shall be elected. The annual meeting of the stockholders of said bank shall thereafter be held on the first Monday of July of each year; but meetings may be called at any time by any number of persons holding one third in value of the stock, by giving similar notice to that prescribed in the first section hereof; and in all meetings of stockholders, each person shall be entitled to one vote for each share of stock held by him. The directors shall hold their offices for one year and until their successors are elected, but may be removed by any regular or called meeting of the stockholders. They may choose one of their number, or of the other stockholders, as their president; may appoint a cashier, and such other officers as they may require, fix their compensation, prescribe their duties, and take bond and security for the faithful discharge thereof; may prescribe the manner and terms of paying in the stock; and shall have the general control of all the affairs of the bank.

**1866.**

Term of office.
President to be chosen.

§ 5. The directors shall annually appoint two or more stockholders to attend and hold the annual elections.

Who to hold elections.

§ 6. At each annual meeting of the stockholders a committee shall be appointed, who shall, at least four times in a year, examine into and report upon the condition of the institution, which report shall be filed in its office and recorded in the minute book, and shall be subject to the inspection of all the stockholders.

Committee to be appointed to examine condition of bank.

§ 7. The business of the bank shall be to receive on deposit United States legal tender notes and such other bank notes that may be current (and it may allow the depositors such interest thereon as may be agreed on); to deal in gold and silver coin, bullion, Kentucky and United States stocks or bonds, bills of exchange, and promissory notes; to buy and sell the stock of other companies; but it shall not issue notes or bills or certificates of deposit, or any other instrument intended for circulation as money; all promissory notes purchased or discounted by said bank, payable at the same or at any other banking institution in this State, shall be put on the footing of foreign bills of exchange; the directors shall annually or semi-annually declare dividends of the profits arising from the business of the bank; the president of the corporation shall cause to be paid into the treasury of this State, on or before the first day of January in each year, fifty cents on each one hundred dollars of stock actually paid in, which shall be in full of all tax or bonus, to be applied towards the sinking fund.

Business of the bank.

§ 8. The said bank shall receive on deposit all sums of money offered during banking hours. Should any person make a deposit in said bank, and be refused payment thereof on demand made for the same during the banking hours, and according to the terms of deposit, and without lawful excuse, said bank shall forfeit and pay to the depositor the full value or amount of his deposit, with damages at the rate of fifteen per centum per annum for the detention, to be recovered by suit in the Pulaski circuit court.

## CHAPTER 531.
1866.

AN ACT to incorporate the Hillsboro and Crain Creek turnpike road company.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That a company is hereby formed for the purpose of making a turnpike road from the town of Hillsboro, in the county of Fleming, to the mouth of Crain creek, or near there. The capital stock of said company shall not exceed fifteen thousand dollars, divided into shares of fifty dollars each, and subscription may be taken up by each of the commissioners hereinafter named, for four years after the passage of this act, unless the whole amount shall be sooner taken up.

§ 2. The following persons are hereby appointed commissioners to perform the duties required by this act, viz: James M. McGrigor, John Myers, Samuel Newman, Samuel Phelps, Z. R. Evans, R. L. McGrigor, J. W. Crain, and J. Turner.

§ 3. The said commissioners, or as many of them as may act, shall procure one or more books or subscription papers, in which the persons taking stock shall bind and oblige themselves, in writing, to pay the Hillsboro and Crain creek turnpike road company, by which name and style said company shall be known, the sum of fifty dollars for each share subscribed for by them, in such proportion and at such times as shall be determined by said company or corporation.

§ 4. That so soon as one hundred shares shall have been subscribed in said capital stock by individuals and corporations, the commissioners shall give ten days' notice, in two public places in said county, of the fact, and appoint a time and place for a meeting of the stockholders in the town of Hillsboro, who shall by vote elect a president and five directors to conduct the business of said corporation, by the name and style aforesaid; the president and directors, and all other officers of said company, shall severally take an oath before some justice of the peace, faithfully to execute the duties of their respective offices; they shall, so soon as may be necessary, proceed to locate said road, and may be authorized to receive the written consent of any of the land-holders, through which or near which said road may pass; and the grant of the right of way, lumber, stone, soil, and timber, to make said road, and keep the same in repair, which consent, when so given, shall be valid and effectual to every intent and purpose.

§ 5. That said road shall be opened and graded at least twenty feet nor more than forty feet wide, and the artificial part thereof covered with stone shall not be less than twelve feet wide; the elevation of the grade of the road shall be fixed and regulated by the president and the directors of the company.

§ 6. That when said road shall be opened and graded for any distance thereof equal to two and one half miles, and a proper and sufficient coating of macadamized stone placed thereon, it shall be lawful for said company to erect a half gate on the same, and to collect for the travel on said road the same rates of toll which are charged and allowed to be collected by the provisions of an act to charter the Hillsboro and

§ 6. The board of directors shall, as soon as practicable after their election, on each occasion, organize by taking an oath of office, and by choosing one of their number to be president of the corporation, who shall preside at all meetings of the board and of the stockholders, if present; and in case of his absence a president *pro tempore* may be chosen from among the other directors. They shall also appoint a secretary and treasurer, and such other officers and employes for the company as may be necessary; and they may require any officer to give bond and security for the faithful discharge of his duty, and to secure any money or property of the company passing through his hands. The board of directors shall manage the general, fiscal, and prudential concerns of the company, and make all the necessary rules and by-laws for the government of the company; may fill all vacancies occurring in their own body during their term of service, and dismiss any officer or employe, not president or a director, at pleasure. Four directors, or three directors and the president, shall be a quorum to do business; but the president shall not vote except to give the casting vote in case of a tie. All orders of the board, and all deeds, notes, bills, bonds, or receipts or other papers of the company shall be signed by the president, and countersigned by the secretary, unless the board of directors shall in any instance, by general or special directions, otherwise order upon the records of the board. The secretary shall attend all meetings of the board, and keep a true record of such meetings, and perform such other clerical duties as the board may require.

§ 7. No subscription of stock shall be received unless accompanied by the payment of twenty per centum thereon at the time of subscription; and the board of directors may call for the remainder in installments not to exceed twenty per centum each, by notifying the subscribers by notice published in some newspaper having general circulation in the cities of Newport, Covington, and Cincinnati, Ohio, ten days' notice before the call shall become due, or by a personal notice of ten days: *Provided*, That at least thirty days shall intervene between calls. In case any stockholder shall fail for three days after his installment shall become due to pay the same, his stock and the payments made thereon may be forfeited by order of the board, or the company may sue for and recover the same with interest.

§ 8. Dividends of the profits of the company shall be declared semi-annually, when the business of the company will admit, at such times as the board of directors shall prescribe and fix; and the board of directors may, unless instructed

§ 3. ## CHAPTER 567.

AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Cox's Creek and Samuels' Depot turnpike road company."

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Cox's Creek and Samuels' Depot turnpike road company," approved February 28, 1860, be, and the same is hereby, so amended that the said company shall hereafter be known and called the "Fairfield and Samuels' Depot turnpike road company;" by which name it shall sue and be sued, and shall have all the rights, privileges, and franchises granted by said act of incorporation.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company is hereby increased to thirty thousand dollars.

§ 3. If any stockholder shall fail to pay the stock subscribed by him, or any part thereof, after notice as prescribed by the eighth section of said act of incorporation, said company may sue for the same in any court in this Commonwealth having jurisdiction thereof.

§ 4. This act shall be in force from its passage.

Approved February 14, 1866.

thecountyof Mercer for the year 1862, appointed by the
AuditorofPublicAccounts,under the laws of thisState;and
whereas,in consequence of the conditionof Mercer county
duringtheyear 1862,and subsequentyears,arisingout of the
warthenexisting, saidPalmer could not collectthe revenue
andmilitarytaxes within the time prescribedby law ; and
whereas,saidPalmer has been compelled topay intothetreas-
urythesum of three hundred and ninety-nine dollarsand
fifty-sevencents,as interestupon the revenueof 1862,and one
hundredand twenty -nine dollarsand twenty centsas interest
onthemilitarytax collectablein thatcounty,none of which
interestwas collectedby saidPalmer ; therefore,
Beitenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth
ofKentucky:
§1.That the Auditor of Public Accounts be ,and is hereby ,
directedto draw his warrant upon the treasurerin favor of
saidPalmer for the sum of fivehundred and twenty- eight
dollarsand seventy-seven cents,the aggregateamount of in-
terestpaidby saidPalmer as setforthin the preamble to this

CHAPTER 584 .
AN ACT for the benefit of the mechanics of Barren and Meade counties .
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :
§1. That carpenters,joiners,brickand stone-masons,plas-
terers,turners, painters,brick-makers ,lumber merchants ,and
allothersperforming laboror furnishingmaterialfor the con-
structionor repairof any building within the counties of
Barren and Meade ,shalland may have a jointlienupon the
buildingsthey may be employed to constructor repair, or for
which they furnishmaterials,to the extentof the labor done
and materialsfurnishedby them respectively: Provided, That
no lien shallattach for any sum not amounting to twenty
dollars .
§ 2. That any person or persons having a lienunder this
act may enforcethe same by filinga petitionin the circuit
court of either of said counties where said lien may exist , at
any timewithinoneyear from thecompletionofthework orfurnishingthematerial. All personshavinga lienmay join
in the petitionagainstthe employer,or one or more of them
may filea petitionagainstthe employer and the other persons
having a lien; each claim shallbe clearlyand distinctly
stated, and as clear and distinct an answer shall be made to
the same . The court,on the applicationof eitherparty,may
direct an issueto be made ,and a jury shalltry the same as
in other cases at law ; and when the claim of each shall be
ascertained, the court shall decree the amount against the
owner of the building and direct the house and the interest
of the employer in the lot, or so much thereofas may be

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 491
necessary,to be sold on a creditof six,twelve,and eighteen
months , and shallcause the salemoney to be collectedand
distributedin payment of the amount decreed to the claim-
ants; the amount due claimantsshallbear legalinterestfrom
the time the same shallhave become due by the contractof
the parties; and the purchaser of the property shallpay in-
terest from the time of the sale. The court, after the claim-
ants have allbeen paid,shall directthe surplus to be paid
over to the owner of the property; and should the proceeds
of the saleprove insufficientto pay allthe claimants,then the
courtshallcauseeach to be paid in proportion.
§3 . That any journeyman who may be employed in the
construction or repair of any building within the counties of
Barren and Meade shall, to the extent of his wages done on
such building,have a lienon the same : Provided, That such
journeyman shallgive to the owner of the property written
noticebefore payment ismade to the undertakeror building
mechanic that he looks to the propertyfor payment of what
may be due to him forlabordone on thebuilding; he may file
hispetitionto enforcethe lien; he may be and shallhave the
right to be made a defendant to any petitionbrought to en-
force any lien given by this act; he shallhave a right to a
decree for the wages due to him, and the same shallbe paid
out of what is due to the undertaker or building mechanic , if
so much be due to him ; and if there be more than one jour-
neyman entitledto a lien under this act,and the amount due
to the undertaker shallnot be sufficientto pay them all,they
shallbe paid in proportion.
§ 4. That none of the personsnamed in the firstsectionof
this act shallhave a lienwhen he has taken security; nor
shall any journeyman have a lien when his principalhas
none .
§ 5. That thecomplainantorcomplainantsinany suitunder
the provisionsof this act may proceed to the proof of their
respectiveclaims immediately on the serviceof process; and
any defendant or defendantsclaiming a lienmay proceed to
the proof of theirrespectiveclaimsimmediately on the filing
of their respectiveanswers assertingand specifyingtheir
claims . It shallnot be necessaryfora defendantor defend-
ants claiming a lienor reliefagainst any complainant or co-
defendant to make their answers or answer a cross-petition
against thecomplainantor any of theco-defendants; but the
assertion of theirrespectiveclaimsand liensin theiranswers,
with a prayer for the appropriateor general relief, shallbe
sufficientto authorizethe courtto enterthe proper decree for
relief against any of the complainantsor defendants.
§ 6. That immediatelyon the serviceof processinany suit
under the provisionsof thisact,any defendantor defendants
shall have a right to filewith the clerk of the circnitcourt of
the county in which saidsuitmay be institutedhisor their
answer under oath ; and the clerk shall indorse thereon the
true time of filingthe same ; and twenty days shallbe allowed
to a defendant or defendants to filetheir answers afterservice
of process. The causeshallstandfortrialat thefirstterm
1866 .

492 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1866 . afterthe serviceof process,ifthe same shallhave been served
twenty days before the commencement of the court, and
eitherat a common law or chancery term,and may be taken
up fortrialon tendays'notice,in writing,on any day ofany
such term. The court, forgood cause, may continuethe
action, and permit a defendant or defendants to filehisor
theiranswers at any time beforethe trialof the cause,on such
terms as the court may deem equitable.
§ 7. This act take effectand be of forcefrom and afterits
passage .
ApprovedFebruary15,1866.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
## CHAPTER 601.
### AN ACT to incorporate the Central Bank.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

**§ 1.** That there is hereby established a bank of deposit in the city of Louisville, with a capital of five hundred thousand dollars, in shares of one hundred dollars each, to be subscribed and paid for as hereinafter specified; and the subscribers and their associates, successors, and assigns, shall be a body-politic and corporate, by the name and style of the "Central Bank;" and shall be capable of contracting and being contracted with, of suing and being sued, of impleading and being impleaded, of answering and defending, in all courts and places whatever; it may have a common seal, and change and renew the same at pleasure.

**§ 2.** Said bank shall be under the control and direction of six directors, each of whom shall be stockholders; they shall be residents of this State, and, after the first election, shall be elected annually on the first Monday in January in each year, and hold office until their successors are elected and qualified; they shall elect one of their number president, and shall hold regular meetings at such times as may be fixed by the by-laws, and other meetings may be held whenever deemed necessary; and in case of the death or resignation of any director, the vacancy shall be filled by the board of directors. The directors shall have power to receive subscriptions for so many of the shares of the stock of said company remaining unsold as they may at any time choose to sell; to declare dividends of the profits arising out of the business; and to appoint such officers, agents, and servants as they may deem necessary to conduct the business of the bank, and pay them such sums as wages and salaries, and take from them such bonds to secure the faithful performance of their duties, as they may think proper and reasonable. The stock shall be deemed personal property, and shall be assignable according to such rules as the board of directors shall, from time to time, establish; but said corporation shall have a lien on the stock to secure any indebtedness by the stockholder to said corporation.

**§ 3.** George W. Morris, Martin Bijur, J. M. Armstrong, Charles Miller, Robert A. Watts, Henry Dent, W. L. Murphy, and M. M. Green, are hereby appointed commissioners, any two of whom may open books and receive subscriptions for the capital stock; and when one thousand shares of stock shall have been subscribed for, it shall be their duty to give notice in two or more daily papers published in said city of Louisville, and appoint a day for the election of a board of directors, who shall hold their offices until the ensuing annual election. The payment for the shares subscribed shall be made as follows: Five dollars on each share at the time of subscribing, and forty-five dollars within ten days after the election of the first board of directors, and the residue in installments of twenty-five dollars on each share every thirty days: *Provided, That after one hundred thousand dollars shall*

Penalty on officers for making false entries, &c.

§ 9. If any cashier, clerk, teller, or other officer, shall appropriate any of the funds of said corporation to his own use, or shall willfully fail to make correct entries, or knowingly make false ones on the books of the bank, with intent to cheat or defraud the corporation or any other person, or to conceal any improper appropriation of funds, the officer so offending shall be deemed guilty of felony, and shall, upon conviction thereof, be sentenced to confinement in the jail and penitentiary of this Commonwealth for a period of not less than two nor more than twenty years.

§ 10. It shall not be lawful for said bank to issue any note or bill to be passed and used as currency.

§ 11. The General Assembly shall have the right to examine the affairs of said corporation by any committee they may appoint for that purpose.

§ 12. This act shall take effect from its passage, and continue in force for twenty years.

Approved February 15, 1866.

## CHAPTER 605.
AN ACT for the benefit of George W. Darlington, formerly sheriff of Greenup county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the further time of two years be allowed George W. Darlington, formerly sheriff of Greenup county, to collect his fee bills and uncollected taxes, during which time said fee bills and taxes may be distrained for as now provided by law; said Darlington shall be liable to all pains and penalties prescribed by law for the issual or collecting of illegal fee bills or taxes.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved February 15, 1866.
