## CHAPTER 613.

AN ACT to incorporate the town of London, in Laurel county.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the town of London, in Laurel county, be, and the same is hereby, incorporated, the corporate limits of which shall be the same as the original plat of said town, including the extensions heretofore made, as also to include the seminary grounds.

§ 2. That there shall be elected by the qualified voters of said town five trustees, any three of whom may form a board to transact business, and who shall, before they enter upon the discharge of their duties, take an oath before some justice of the peace for said county that they will faithfully and impartially discharge the duties of the office aforesaid.

§ 3. That said trustees and their successors in office shall be a body-politic and corporate, and shall be known by the name and style of the “Board of Trustees of the town of London;” and by that name shall be capable of contracting and being contracted with, of suing and being sued, pleading and being impleaded, answering and being answered, of defending and being defended, in all courts of this Commonwealth; and have and exercise all the rights and privileges which incorporated bodies may lawfully do for the purpose contemplated by this act.

of Kentucky:
§1. That B. F. Reynolds,James W. Bradley,and Hugh M.
Bradley,and theirassociates,successors,and assigns,be,and

withsuccessionforthirtyyears,by thename and styleofthe
"ReynoldsMining and ManufacturingCompany ;" with all

topurchase and hold lands,in fee simple and by lease, not

saidlands,or buildany or allkinds of floats, mills, or other
buildings, fixturesand machinery, which said corporation

tion, or dispose of any portion or portionsof its territory
and other propertydeemed advisable,and convey the same
by deed or otherwise.

1866 . § 3. The capitalstockofthiscompany shallnot exceedfivehundred thousand dollars,and shallbe divided into sharesof

dollars,and may be issued and transferredin such way and

ble .

dent, who shallbe electedby a majority of interestof the

the presidentshallrefuseor declineto act,or resignafter

untilthenextelectionby thestockholders. The trusteesmay ,

lawsand rulesforthemanagement of theproperty,business,
and affairsof the company as they may deem proper,not in-
consistentwith the laws and Constitutionof this State; they

ent,and such other officersas they may deem necessary, with

CHAPTER 632 .
AN ACT to prohibitthe sale of spirituousliquorsin thetown of Monticello,
Wayne county,or withintwo miles of the court-house of saidtown.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
§ 1. That from and afterthe firstday of April, 1866,no
licenseshallbe granted by the judges of the Wayne county
court,or by the board of trusteesof the town of Monticello,
Kentucky ,to vend or sellspirituous,malt,or vinous liquors,
by retail, withinsaidtown ,or within two milessquare of the
court-house in said town : Provided, That druggists and
apothecariesmay be licensedwithoutfee,by the trusteesof
said town ,to sellthe same for medical purposes alone,upon
the personorpersonssoapplyingforlicensetakingan oath

550 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1866 . before the chairman of the board of trustees of said town to
the effectthat they,or any person within theiremploy ,will
not sellspirituousorother liquorstoany personexcept on the
written order of some residentpractisingphysician in the
county, of sobrietyand in good standing in his profession,
statingthe date of the order,the person to whom to be sold,
the amount tobe sold,thatit has been prescribeby him as a
medicine,thatthe order is given by him in good faith,and
signedby the physician,and the order shallonly be good for
once selling.
§ 2. Any person sellinglessthan 10 gallonsat any one time
shallbe deemed a retailerwithin the meaning of thisact; and
when ten gallonsissold,itshallallbe removed atthe time of
purchase and sale; and ifany part of itisleftat the place of
salewith the knowledge or connivanceof the seller, shall
constitute him a retailer.
§ 3. Any person violatingthe provisionsof thisact shallbe
fined a sum not lessthan $50 nor more than $100 ,in the dis-
cretionof a jury,and a capiaspro finemay issueinstanteron
the judgment ; one fourthof the fineto go to the use of the
informer, one fourthto the county attorney,and the remain-
der to the trusteesof saidtown ,to be expended on the streets
of said town .
§ 4.Any personwho shallattempt,by any devicewhatso-
ever, to evade the provisionsof thisact,shallbe deemed a re-
tailer,and any physicianwho shallgive an order forliquor
forthe purpose of evading thisact,or who shallgive such
order without being fullysatisfiedof itsnecessityas a medi-
cine,shallbe liableto the same punishment as ifhe had sold
the liquor himself . :
§5 . Where any licenseto retailspirituousor otherliquors
has been granted to any person or persons,and do not expire
beforethe firstday of Aprilnext,the board of trusteesof said
town ,or a majorityof them ,shallhave fullpower and au-
thority,ifin theiropinionthe peace and morals of the com-
munity requireit,to enterup an ordervacatingand declaring
such licensenulland void; but beforemaking such order they
shallrefund to the person or personswhose licenseis so va-
cated the amount of money overpaid by them , and the person
whose license is vacated shall thereafter be liable to all the
penaltiesprovided in thisact againstretailing.§ 6.The countyjudge of Wayne shallhave jurisdiction
concurrent with the circuit court of all offenses against this
act,and an appeal may be taken by eitherparty to the circuit
court : Provided, That when taken to the circuitcourt,the at-
torney forthe Commonwealth shallnot superintendthe case.
§ 7. Prosecutionsunder thisact shallbe in the name of the
board of trusteesof the town of Monticello,Kentucky .
§ 8. This actshallnot be repealed except upon a petition
of a majorityof thequalifiedvotersof thetown.
§ 9. This actto be in forcefrom itspassage.
ApprovedFebruary16,1866.

CHAPTER 634 .
AN ACT forthe benefitof the suretiesofL. D. Owen ,latesheriffofHenry
county .
WHEREAS ,Joseph Brinker and John L. Jenkins were the
suretiesof L. D. Owen , latea sheriffof Henry county, for
the collectionof the revenue of saidcounty forthe year 1862,
and that said revenue amounted to the sum of $15,656 21;
that Owen collecteda portionthereof,not exceeding $2,500,
abandoned hisoffice,and leftthe State, without having paid
into the treasury any of the revenue due from the county,
whereby hissuretiesbecame liableforhis defalcation,which
they have sincepaid; thatin theirsettlementmade with the
Auditor,they were charged with,and have paid as intereston
said defalcation, the sum of $ 427 33,and that in said settle-
ment interestwas charged for several months before the tax
books came to the hands of said Owen ; and whereas , it also
appearing thatbeforeand at the time saidOwen abandoned
his office,the county was in such a conditionthat collections
of revenue could not be safelymade ,and that,in consequence
of the unexpected departureof saidsheriff, the suretiescould
not ascertain the amount for which he was liable,and that so
soon as the amount was ascertainedthey paidit,togetherwith
the amount of interestabove stated, into the treasury, for all
ofwhich payments the estateof the saidOwen willnotre-
imburse them in more than one sixthof the amount paid for
him ; forremedy whereof,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Auditor draw his warrant on the treasurer in
favor of the said Brinker and Jenkins for the sum of four
hundred and twenty sevendollarsand thirty-threecents,the
amount of interestso paid by them .
§ 2. This actto be in forcefrom and afteritspassage.
ApprovedFebruary16,1866.

thousand dollars of said stock is subscribed, and twenty per cent. thereof paid in, notice shall be given of the time and place of holding an election for a board of trustees and president, who shall be elected by a majority of interest of the stockholders represented. If any of those elected trustees, or the president, shall refuse or decline to act, or resign after acceptance, a majority of those accepting may fill the vacancy until the next election by the stockholders. The trustees may, from time to time, adopt, alter, repeal, and amend such by-laws and rules for the general management of the property, business, and affairs of the company as they may deem proper, not inconsistent with the laws and constitution of this State. They may keep their offices at such place or places as they may deem best for the interest of the corporation. They may appoint and remove a secretary, treasurer, superintendent, and such other officers as they may deem necessary, with such compensation for services as they may fix. They may make such calls of payment of subscriptions for stock as they may deem proper, not to exceed twenty per cent. for every thirty days; and in default of payment, the by-laws of said company may authorize a forfeiture of stock. They may use a common seal, and alter the same at pleasure; and they may require a bond, with security, from such officers as may be elected or appointed. Until the election hereinbefore provided for, the powers and functions vested in said board of trustees are vested in said corporators. The company shall have and keep an office and agent in this State.

§ 6. This act to take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 16, 1866.

terred, and make such other by-laws and regulations as may be necessary for the purposes of the corporation; they shall have full power to enter upon and remove any ornaments, fixtures, or shrubbery that may be placed on or around the graves against the by-laws and regulations of the corporation; they shall have the power to appoint, from time to time, such superintendent or other officers as they may think necessary, and take from them such bond as may be required. The proceeds of the sale of lots and all money that may come to the corporation from any other source, shall be applied, first to reimburse those who have made advancements for the original outlay and purchase of the establishment, and shall afterwards, in all time to come, be applied to ornament and improve the grounds and defray incidental expenses.

§ 4. When a burial lot is purchased the trustees shall give a certificate thereof, under the seal of the corporation, which shall vest the purchaser with title. This title may be transferred according to such rules and regulations as may be prescribed by the by-laws of the corporation, but in no other manner. If not transferred by the grantee, it shall descend or pass by devise as other real estate. Such lots shall never be used for any other purpose than burial lots; and if applied to any other use, the title shall revert to the corporation.

§ 5. If any person shall forcibly, and without lawful authority, violate any of the graves of the dead, or deface any of the tombstones, monuments, or inclosures, or injure any of the grounds, shrubbery, fixtures, or buildings, or in any manner damage the grounds of the corporation, such person or persons so offending, besides being liable to an indictment for a misdemeanor, and punishable according to the discretion of a jury, shall be liable to the corporation in an act of trespass, and the damages, when recovered, shall be applied by the corporation to restore, as far as possible, any injury that has been done.

§ 6. This act shall take effect and be in force from and after its passage.

Approved February 16, 1866.

eralgrantsand privileges,toan extentnecessaryforthebusinessandobjectoftofthiscorporation:Provided.Thatthecom-
pany shallatno timeholdlandsexceedingonethousandacres
in extent;may sell,convey,ortransferthesametoanyper-sonorpersonsorcorporations;mayhave,hold,andpossessoa

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.
587

of the business of said company; may make all needful rules and regulations for the government and management of the business of the company, from time to time, as may be deemed right and proper.

**1866.**

§ 2. The object of said company is to develop the petroleum, rock, and carbon oils, iron, coal, and other minerals in Southern Kentucky; to that end may acquire, own, and possess, by purchase or otherwise, lands and leases and mineral grants and privileges in that part of the State; may develop the same by boring for oil, salt water, or other liquids; may sink shafts, open mines for all manner of minerals thereon; may refine said oils, manufacture salt and all kinds of minerals, transport the same to market in the raw or manufactured state; may erect cooper-shops, machine and manufacturing-shops of all kinds and descriptions; may sink wells, establish depots, make and own landings, wharves, and boats of all descriptions; may make roads of any kind necessary for the speedy and economical transportation of their works to the nearest accessible highway or navigable river: *Provided*, That the entire property at any time owned by said company shall not exceed the capital stock of the corporation and twenty per cent. additional.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall not exceed one million dollars, to be divided into shares of not less than ten nor more than one hundred dollars each, to be issued in such manner as may be prescribed by the by-laws of said company; the shares of said company shall be transferable in person or by attorney upon the books of the company, which books, as well as the stock and other books of said company, shall at all times be open to the inspection of any stockholder; the shares of said company shall be deemed and held as personal property.

§ 4. The affairs of said company shall be conducted by a board of directors not exceeding five in number, one of whom shall be president of the company, all of whom shall be stockholders. Said board shall consist of the corporators herein, who shall continue in office until their successors are elected by a majority of the stockholders in interest; the board of directors shall be elected annually, and shall continue in office one year or until their successors are duly elected; and in case of vacancy, produced from any cause, the remaining directors shall have power to supply such vacancies, from time to time, as they may occur.

§ 5. So soon as $20,000 of the stock shall be subscribed for in money, the corporators herein may meet at such time and place as they may deem proper, and a majority being present, may organize said company and proceed with the business of the company; may make by-laws, rules and regulations, for the thorough and efficient organization thereof; may direct the issuing of stock, determine the amount thereof, receive therefor the payment; and they and their successors may appoint all such necessary officers and agents for conducting the business thereof; shall select a secretary and treasurer, define their duties, may require bond of them for faithful per-

Digitized by Google

588
# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

1866. formance of their duties, and may fix the compensation for the services of all such officers and agents; may prescribe the mode in which the books and papers of said company shall be kept; and do all other acts necessary for the successful working of the business of the company.

§ 6. The principal office shall be at the city of Frankfort, Kentucky, subject to removal by order of the directors; may have branch offices at any other points which said board may designate.

§ 7. Each stockholder shall have as many votes as he has shares in said company; may vote by proxy or otherwise. They shall annually elect their directors, and the secretary shall give twenty days' notice (published in some newspaper in Kentucky) of the time and place of said election.

§ 8. No banking privileges are allowed by this charter; nor shall the directors have power to establish any by-law or regulation inconsistent with the Constitution or laws of the United States or of the State of Kentucky.

§ 9. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved February 16, 1866.

## CHAPTER 664.
### AN ACT to incorporate the Cairo and Caseyville Mining and Manufacturing Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

**Corporators' names and corporate powers.** § 1. That Nicholas W. Casey, Samuel L. Casey, Henry Glass, William H. Green, and associates, be, and are hereby, created a body corporate and politic, by the name and style of the “Cairo and Caseyville Mining and Manufacturing Company;” may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in all courts and places; have a common seal, and alter and break the same at pleasure; may make all necessary by-laws for the transaction of the business and government of said company, not inconsistent with the Constitution and laws of this State or the United States.

**Capital stock.** § 2. The capital stock of said company shall not exceed four millions of dollars, which may be divided into shares, subscribed and paid for, in such manner as said company may by its by-laws prescribe.

**Who to manage.** § 3. That said company shall have power to appoint any one or more of its members, or other persons, to manage, control, and direct the business of said company, according to the by-laws, rules and regulations, which said company may adopt.

**May own real estate.** § 4. That said company may acquire and hold such real and personal estate, not exceeding three hundred thousand dollars, by contract or otherwise, as they may deem necessary or proper for carrying on the mining of coal, iron, lead, or other minerals; the procuring and manufacturing or refining of oil, salt, and lumber, and the transportation thereof to market; together with such quantities of land as may be necessary for

## CHAPTER 666.

AN ACT to authorize the county court of Bourbon county to levy a tax to build a bridge across Hinkson creek.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky*:

§ 1. That the county court of Bourbon county, a majority of the justices of the peace then in commission being present, and a majority of those present assenting and concurring, may appropriate a sum, not exceeding five thousand dollars, to aid in the erection of a bridge across Hinkson creek at a point where the Paris and Jackstown turnpike touches it: *Provided*, Sufficient funds to complete the same be raised by the county of Nicholas and private individuals or corporations.

§ 2. For the purpose of paying said subscription, said court, at the same time and in the same manner, levy a tax, not exceeding five cents on the hundred dollars, upon all the property in said county subject to taxation for revenue purposes, to be collected by the sheriff of said county at the same time and in the same manner other taxes are collected by him; and said sheriff shall pay the same when collected to the treasurer of said county, who shall pay over the same to three commissioners, who shall be appointed by the judge of said court to let, contract for, and superintend the building of said

county of Nicholas; said commissioners shallexecutebond,
with good security,before saidjudge ,payable to the Com-
monwealth , conditioned for the faithful discharge of their
duties, which may be sued on for any breach of itscondi.
tions .
§ 3. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved February16,1866.

## CHAPTER 669.

AN ACT to discontinue an alley in the town of Catlettsburg.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the alley of fourteen feet in what is called the new survey or addition to the town of Catlettsburg, beginning on Webster street and running between and parallel with Louisa and Panola streets, be closed: Provided, That it will not interfere with the convenience of said town: Provided, That a majority of the board of trustees of said town shall first consent to the closing of said alley, and no private right injured thereby.

§ 2. This act to take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 16, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 670.

AN ACT to change the name of the Reverdy Petroleum and Manufacturing Company.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the name and style of “The Reverdy Petroleum and Manufacturing Company,” incorporated by an act, entitled “An act to incorporate the Reverdy Petroleum and Manufacturing Company,” approved March 1, 1865, be, and the same is hereby, changed to, and shall hereafter be known and styled, “The Ohio and Mississippi Steamboat Coal Company.”

§ 2. That this act take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 16, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 673.

AN ACT for the benefit of H. Helm, late sheriff of Lincoln county.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:

§ 1. That the further time of two years be, and the same is hereby, given to H. Helm, late sheriff of Lincoln county, in which to collect his unpaid tax and fee bills: Provided, He shall have the right to distrain for the same, and be subject to all the pains and penalties now in force for the collection of illegal taxes and fee bills.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from its passage.

Approved February 16, 1866.

Upon the execution of such writ or writs, the jurors shall view the ground, stone quarries, beds of gravel, and other material requested to be condemned; and if, in their opinion, it shall be necessary and proper to condemn said ground, stone quarries, beds of gravel, and other material, they shall say so by their inquest, and lay off and designate such ground or other material, and shall assess the value thereof, which inquest shall be returned to the county court of Lewis and recorded, upon which, if said company, or any one of them, pay into said court the value of the land or other thing so assessed by the jury, it shall vest the land or other property in the said company in fee simple.

of Kentucky :

Corporators' P.Gray,JamesM. Moore,Hudson Johnson,H. S.Moore.W.
poratepowers. P. Griffey,RobertHunt ,and theirassociates,successors,and

lumbusManufacturingand TradingCompany;" andbythat

in thisCommonwealth; may have and usea commonseal,

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY.

tions; may have, hold, and transfer any personal property necessary for the transaction of the business of said corporation; may do a general trading business in all kinds and characters of goods, wares, and merchandise, purchasing and selling same, receiving and forwarding business; may do a general manufacturing business: Provided, The company herein incorporated shall not own or hold land for any other than manufacturing purposes, and the land so owned or held shall never at any one time exceed in value fifty thousand dollars.

§ 2. Said corporation shall be under the control and direction of five directors, each of whom shall be stockholders and residents of the State of Kentucky. They shall elect one of their number president, and shall hold regular meetings at such times as may be fixed by the by-laws; and other meetings may be had when deemed necessary; and in case of death or resignation of any director, the vacancy shall be filled by the board of directors. They shall have power to receive subscriptions for stock in said company as they may choose to sell; to declare dividends; to appoint such officers, agents, and servants as they may deem necessary to conduct the business of the corporation, and pay them such sums as wages and salaries, and take from them such bond to secure the faithful performance of their duties, as they shall think proper and reasonable. The stock shall be deemed personal property, and shall be assignable according to such rules as the board of directors shall, from time to time, establish; but said company shall have a lien on the stock to secure any indebtedness by the stockholder.

§ 3. That five of the above named persons may meet and organize by electing a president, and they and their successors shall have power to pass by-laws for the government of said corporation; to open books and receive subscriptions for the capital stock, and when such amount of capital stock shall have been subscribed as may be fixed by the by-laws, said corporation may commence operations; and the payment of said stock and forfeiture for non-payment shall be fixed by the by-laws.

§ 4. That each share of stock shall represent one hundred dollars, and each share represent one vote.

§ 5. That the directory may at any time be changed by a call made upon the president for that purpose by a majority of all the stock, when a new directory shall be elected, each share representing a vote, which may be cast by direct vote or by proxy, and the old board shall hold said election.

§ 6. No one shall become indebted to the corporation until the whole amount of stock taken by him has been paid up; nor shall any stockholder transfer his stock without the consent of the directory, unless to another stockholder.

§ 7. The main establishment shall be located in the town of Columbus, Kentucky, and the directory shall have power to establish branches at such place or places as they may determine.

§ 8. That the stockholders shall not be liable for any greater sum than the amount of stock subscribed; but all the stock

## CHAPTER 691.

AN ACT to incorporate the Mount Savage Coal, Oil, Iron, and Manufacturing Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That R. M. Biggs, Andrew Biggs, William Coleman, and F. K. Farrall, and their associates and successors, be, and they are hereby, created a body corporate and politic, by the name of the "Mount Savage Coal, Oil, Iron, and Manufacturing Company," with all the powers and authority incident to corporators and corporations, for the purposes hereinafter mentioned.

§ 2. The corporation is hereby authorized and empowered to purchase, sell, hold, and dispose of lands, by lease, for mining and boring for petroleum and other oils and minerals, of whatsoever kind, and to manufacture, refine, transport, and vend the same.

§ 3. The capital stock of the corporation shall be divided into shares of not less than five nor more than five hundred dollars each; which shares may be issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions, and under such rules of forfeiture for non-payment of calls and assessments, as the board of directors of such corporation may direct; and the liability of shareholders shall extend to and be limited by the amount of stock held by them respectively.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 611

seven shall constitute a quorum, and when the number is reduced below that number, the remaining members shall wind up the affairs of the society, and distribute its proceeds among such charitable institutions of this Commonwealth as they may choose.

§ 4. A board of no less than five nor more than nine members shall be chosen annually in the month of February, in such way as the by-laws may direct, and shall have the management of the current business of the society.

§ 5. No shares or bonds issued by the association shall entitle their holders to any interest or profit beyond the amount of money actually paid in, with legal interest; and any profit made by the society beyond this, either from the dues or voluntary contributions of members, renting out of parts of its buildings, or otherwise, shall remain as a surplus or sinking fund, subject to final distributions as above mentioned.

§ 6. The society shall exercise no banking powers of any kind, nor ever engage in or carry on any lottery.

§ 7. This act is to go into effect from and after its passage, and the Legislature reserves the right to repeal this act, or so much thereof as it may deem necessary.

Approved February 17, 1866.

## CHAPTER 696.

AN ACT to incorporate the Paris Hotel Association.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That a body-politic and corporate in the city of Paris be hereby created, to be styled the Paris Hotel Association, with a capital of one hundred and fifty thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of fifty dollars each, with power of contracting and being contracted with, of suing and being sued, and the usual powers incident to corporations.

§ 2. Said company shall be authorized to purchase and hold land in the city of Paris, not exceeding five acres, and erect thereon the necessary and proper building for a hotel; and to hold such personal property as may be proper in the management and conducting a hotel, and the same to sell at any time, and make other purchases.

§ 3. That the fiscal and other prudential concerns of said company shall be under the management and control of a president and five managers elected by the stockholders on the first Saturday in January of each year, after the first election, each share giving to a stockholder one vote in all elections, which may be cast in person or by proxy.

§ 4. Said company may appropriate or lease any of the rooms in its hotel building for stores or other purposes.

§ 5. That B. F. Massie, John T. Hinton, B. F. Pullen, and George W. Davis, or any two of them, may, at any time within two years, open books of subscription for stock in said com-

or so much as may be necessaryforthe purposesof saidcom-
pany, shallhave been taken; and when the subscriptions
amount to twenty thousand dollars,said commissioners, or
thoseacting,may , by noticein a newspaper publishedin said
city,calla meeting of the stockholdersin not lessthan fifteen
days,to electa presidentand fivemanagers ; and upon such
electionbeing made ,thisactshallgo intoeffect.
§ 6. Said presidentand managers may determine the time
and installmentsin which the subscriptionsshallbe paid.
§ 7. Saidcompany shallbe authorizedtoborrowmoney,not
exceedingtwenty thousand dollars,and issueitsbonds,secured
by mortgage upon the property-rights and privilegesof the
corporation,forthe repayment of the money borrowed,or sell
such bonds on such time as a majorityin amount ofthe stock-
holders may consent to; said bonds and mortgage shallbe
signedby the presidentand countersignedby the secretaryof
saidcompany ,and under itscorporateseal.
§ 8. Saidpresidentand managers may make such by-laws
and regulationsas they may deem proper, not inconsistent
with law,and appoint such officersas they may deem neces-
sary,and take bond therefromforthe faithfulperformance of
their duties .
§ 9. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved February17,1866.

## CHAPTER 698.

AN ACT to amend "An act to incorporate Gordonsville, Logan county."
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky :*

§ 1. That the second section of an act approved May 24th, 1861, entitled "An act to incorporate the town of Gordonsville, Logan county," be amended as follows: by striking out the second Saturday and inserting first Saturday.
§ 2. By reason of the war, said town has failed to elect trustees for the last three years, and have no officer to hold said election: pray that C. T. Williams and P. A. Lyon, justices of the peace for Gordonsville district, be, and are hereby, appointed judges to hold the first election of trustees; and they are hereby empowered to appoint a clerk and sheriff to assist in holding said election; in case one of the judges herein named cannot be present, then the judge present may appoint one, which must be a voter of Gordonsville district.
§ 3. That the first election of trustees shall be holden on the second Saturday in February next; the trustees elected at said election, shall hold their office until the first Saturday in April, or until their successors are elected and qualified.
§ 4. That nothing in this act shall be construed as repealing the act approved May the 24th, 1861, but the same shall remain in full force and virtue with the amendments herein set forth.
§ 5. This act to take effect from its passage.
Approved February 17, 1866.

## CHAPTER 714.
1866.

AN ACT to remove voting precinct No 11, in Pulaski county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That hereafter all elections held in district No. 11, in Pulaski county, shall be held at Woodstock in said district, instead of Weldon's place.
§ 2. This act shall be in force from and after its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 715.

AN ACT to change the boundary line of voting district No. 1, of Pike county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the boundary line of district No. 1, in Pike county, be so changed as to include the farm and residence of Martin Thornbury.
§ 2. This act to take effect from date of its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 716.

AN ACT for the benefit of the citizens of Tompkinsville.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That in case of death, resignation, or removal of any of the officers or trustees enumerated in said act, said trustees shall have the authority to appoint any person or persons to fill the vacancies so occurring; to hold their office till the next regular election, a majority being a quorum to do business.
§ 2. This act to take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 718.

AN ACT for the benefit of Asa Gilbert, late sheriff of Clay county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That an act for the benefit of Asa Gilbert, approved January, 1865, be, and the same is hereby, so far revived as to give Asa Gilbert the further time till the 1st day of June, 1866, to collect, pay into the Treasury the unpaid revenue for the year 1864, and to return his delinquent list for the years 1861-'62-'63: *Provided, however*, That before they accept the benefit of this act, his sureties shall give their consent to this extension, to be there entered in the Clay county court of record.
§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

The capital stock of said company shall be five hundred thousand dollars, which may be lessened or increased to not exceeding three millions of dollars, which may be divided into shares of such amount as the company may deem advisable, and which may be subscribed and paid for in real estate, leases for, or conveyances of oil and mineral privileges, cash, Government or State bonds, or in such other manner as said company may deem proper; and which stock may be issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions as the board of directors of said company may direct; and which shares shall be deemed personal estate.

Capital stock.

§ 4. That it shall be lawful for the president and directors of said company, from time to time, to borrow money, or obtain on loan such sums of money, and on such terms, as they may deem expedient for the prosecution of the business of the company: *Provided*, Such sum or sums shall never exceed one half of its capital stock; and to pledge or mortgage any of the real estate, improvements, privileges, effects, or assets whatever of said company for the repayment of said sums so borrowed, and may issue any character of bond or other evidence of responsibility or debt they think proper, or dispose of any portion or portions of its territory or other property deemed advisable, and convey the same by deed or otherwise.

May borrow money.

§ 5. The affairs of the company shall be managed by three directors, one of whom shall be president, and all of whom shall be stockholders in said company; and the first board of directors shall consist of Isaac Caldwell, William Berry, and S. F. J. Trabue, who shall continue in office until their successors are elected by a majority in interest of the stockholders of said company; and the board of directors thus chosen shall continue in office for one year or until their successors are elected. If any of the directors shall decline or refuse to act, or die, either before or after elected by the stockholders, a majority of the others shall fill the vacancy by appointing some one else. ## CHAPTER 735.

AN ACT to incorporate the Alves, Lewis, & Co. Petroleum and Mining Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That H. E. Lewis, W. D. Alves, and James S. Alves, and their successors and associates, are hereby constituted a body-corporate and politic, under the name and style of Alves, Lewis, & Co. Petroleum and Mining Company; and under that name may sue and be sued, purchase and hold lands, by lease or in fee simple, not exceeding in value one hundred thousand dollars.

§ 2. The corporators above named shall be and constitute the directors of said corporation until their successors shall be elected and qualified; and they may elect one of their own number as president, and may elect a secretary and treasurer who may be of their own number or not, as they may determine.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall consist of ninety thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of not less than ten dollars each, but may be increased at any time, by order of the directors, to any amount not exceeding three hundred thousand dollars; said company may, however, commence operations as soon as forty thousand dollars of said capital stock shall be subscribed, and ten per cent. thereof paid in; the remaining portion of said capital stock may be issued and sold by the board of directors of said company at such times as may seem to them best: *Provided*, That every subscriber to the capital stock of said company shall be liable to its creditors for the full amount of his subscription, unless the same shall have been actually paid in money to the company.

§ 4. The manner and time of electing directors, the number of the same, the manner and time of electing secretary, treasurer, and other officers of the corporation, of receiving subscriptions of stock, or of selling stock in the market, and generally all matters pertaining to the business and conduct of said corporation, shall be controlled and regulated by the by-laws of said corporation, from time to time enacted by the directors thereof; and said company shall have and keep an office or agent in this State.

pay the sum of five dollars on each share subscribed, or execute a note for the sum, payable to the president and directors of the Danville and McMinnville railroad company in thirty days from its date; any sum received by any commissioner to be deposited in some banking institution to the credit of said company, designating the name of the person making such payment; and if the company is not formed and operations commenced within twelve months from the date of the deposit, it shall from thence stand to the credit of the person or persons making the same, and subject to their order. The currency of the vicinity where subscriptions are made may be received; and all subscriptions and notes taken may be paid in currency; notes taken will be void and cancelled if operations are not commenced in twelve months. A list of all subscriptions made shall, within three months from the time the books were opened, be forwarded to C. T. Worthington, Frank Wolford, Thomas E. Bramlette, T. T. Alexander, D. R. Haggard, and T. C. Winfrey, at the town of Danville, who shall constitute a central board for receiving subscriptions. Said board shall meet in the town of Danville on the first Monday in June next, to ascertain the whole number of shares subscribed in said company, and publish the same in papers published in the cities of Louisville, Lexington, and Cincinnati. If it shall appear that the number of shares have been subscribed as required herein, said company will be regarded in law as formed, and the said central commission, or a majority of them, shall sign two duplicate lists or declarations of the fact that said subscription had been made; and in like manner may assemble, from time to time, at the said town of Danville, to receive other statements of subscriptions of stock, and upon each transfer duplicate declarations to that effect, with the names of the subscribers appended, and cause said duplicates to be filed in the office of Secretary of State of the State of Kentucky; and thenceforth the said subscribers of stock shall form one body-politic and corporate, in deed and in law, in the State of Kentucky, for the purposes set forth in this act of incorporation.

§ 4. That in case any of the persons forming said central commission shall not attend at Danville on the first Monday in June, 1866, or attending, should refuse or be unable to act, those who do attend shall fill the vacancy.

§ 5. The Danville and McMinnville railroad company, formed as aforesaid, shall have succession of members for ninety years; may sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in any court of law or equity; may have a seal of corporation; and may make all such by-laws, rules and regulations, as are necessary for the government of the corporation, or for effecting the object of its creation: Provided, They are not repugnant to the Constitution and laws of the State of Kentucky and of the United States. Notice or service of process of law may be served on the president or secretary or principal superintendent, shall be held valid in any controversy wherein said company is a party, and, upon such service of process, the said corporation, by its president or agent, shall respond.

1866. be sued, plead and be impleaded, and have a common seal; may make by-laws for its government and perpetuation, and all the corporate privileges herein granted, and subject to the same liabilities, and have and hold all property in common according to the agreed terms, which shall be in writing, signed by the president of each road, countersigned by the secretaries of each board, with the seal of each corporation, and shall be recorded in the clerk's office of the county court of each county through which the road may run; or said company may contract with other companies in Kentucky or Tennessee for a through line of transportation without reshipment from the connecting point with any railroad in Tennessee and Georgia to the Ohio river. On all causes of action prior to the amalgamation hereby authorized, each company shall be sued in its original name.

§ 32. The said company shall keep its business office in the State of Kentucky, but may have agencies within any of the States to facilitate its business operations. The company shall be, and is hereby, made liable for any injury to persons or to property, or other cause of action created within any county through which it may run, in that county where the cause of action accrued. Process may be served on the president, the secretary, or general superintendent, and with a view to such service, process may be sent to any county in the State.

§ 33. In the construction of the road, said company shall, in all instances, so regulate the grade, or so improve any crossing of any public road or private passway, neighborhood, mill, or church road, so as not to obstruct or materially affect travel along said road; and where the road shall pass through the farm of any person, suitable and well constructed cattle guards shall be constructed to prevent stock passing from one field to another; and said company shall in like manner construct roads to pass from one part of the farm to another, where the same are necessary to cross their said road. This duty of the company shall be estimated in the assessment of damages, and for a failure to perform it, a right of action will accrue to the owner of the land.

§ 34. The company shall be liable to the owner for any stock or other damage occasioned by the running of the road by reason of the negligence or carelessness of the operatives on the train doing the damage. For any stock killed or damaged in the inclosure of any person, half the value of the damage done shall be assessed and paid to the owner or person entitled to receive such damage by said company, whether resulting from carelessness or negligence or not.

§ 35. The stock of the company shall be issued to the subscribers of stock on making full payment, or on part payment of the whole sum subscribed under order of the board; but no stock for less than one hundred dollars shall be issued. Transfers of stock shall be made from one person to another in the manner prescribed by the by-laws of the company. All certificates of stock shall be signed by the president, and countersigned by the secretary, and the corporate seal attached.

§ 36. That any company formed under this act be, and they

are vested with the authority and the right, with all the powers herein granted, to extend the line of communication from Danville to the Ohio river, either by the construction of a new railroad, or by the purchase of the corporate rights and all franchise rights, ways and appurtenances, and property of any other railway company or companies; and with a view to effect that object, any such railway company or companies on the line of communication to the Ohio, are hereby vested with the power to sell, transfer, and deliver over to a company hereby formed all its rights aforesaid; and on the making such transfer, the company hereby created shall be completely vested with all the rights and privileges secured by the charters of the companies. The company hereby created shall also have the power and the right to effect a continuous line by contracting with any other company or companies for reciprocal rights of transportation, or any contract that may be necessary to effect a through and continuous line of transportation.

§ 37. That said company may charge for freight and passage, and transportation of every kind and description, the same as allowed to the Lexington and Frankfort railroad company; that is, on the same scale of charges, having due regard to a greater or shorter distance.

§ 38. It shall be the duty of the board of directors to make a semi-annual dividend of the profits of said road, on the first days of January and July in each year; but may retain an amount sufficient to cover contingencies, which sum shall be reported at each meeting of the stockholders, which reserved fund shall not be used by the board for any other purpose than to promote the interest of the stockholders and advance the interests of the company.

§ 39. It shall be the duty of the president, at each annual meeting of the stockholders, to make a printed report of the affairs and conditions of the company. The annual meeting of the stockholders shall be held in the town of Danville, unless otherwise directed by the board; at which meetings, in the election of officers, and all other matters, each stockholder shall, by himself or proxy, cast one vote for each share on which all the calls have been paid. Special meetings may be called at any time on application of one fourth the stockholders in interest.

§ 40. The board shall designate by order in what bank all the funds of the company shall be kept, or direct how the funds shall otherwise be kept; and if kept according to the order of the board, the treasurer or receiver shall not be liable to loss by reason of any loss of the funds; they shall be drawn from the place of deposit only on the check of the president, or vice-president in his absence, or, in case of absence of both, the board shall direct by written order, on the order book, who shall have the right to check for money; all checks shall be countersigned by the secretary of the company.

## CHAPTER 747.
1866.

AN ACT to incorporate the H. E. Lewis Petroleum and Mining Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That H. E. Lewis, W. L. Alves, James S. Alves, J. W. Alves, Geo. A. Lewis, and Jacob F. Weller, and their associates and successors, are hereby constituted a body corporate and politic, under the name and style of the "H. E. Lewis Petroleum and Mining Company;" and under that name may sue and be sued, purchase and hold lands, by lease or in fee simple, not exceeding in value one hundred thousand dollars.

§ 2. The corporators above named shall be and constitute the directors of said corporation until their successors shall be elected and qualified; and they may elect one of their own number as president; and may elect a treasurer and secretary, who may be either of their own number or not, as they may determine.

§ 3. The capital stock of said company shall consist of one hundred thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of not less than ten dollars each, or over one hundred dollars; but the capital stock may be increased at any time, by order of the directors, to any amount not exceeding two hundred thousand dollars. Said company may, however, commence operations whenever sixty thousand dollars of said capital stock be subscribed, and ten per cent. thereof paid in. The remaining portion of said capital stock may be issued and sold by the board of directors of said company, at such times as may seem to them best: *Provided*, That every stockholder shall be liable to any creditor of the company for the full amount of his subscription until he shall have actually paid the same in money to the company.

§ 4. The manner and time of electing directors; the number of the same, the manner and time of electing secretary and treasurer, and other officers of the corporation, of receiving subscriptions of stock, or of selling stock in the market, and generally all matters pertaining to the business and conduct of said corporation, shall be controlled and regulated by the by-laws of said corporation, from time to time enacted by the directors thereof. Said company shall have and keep an office or agent in this State.

§ 5. This charter is limited to fifty years; but the Legislature reserves the right to alter, amend, or repeal the same at pleasure; and is declared to be in force from and after its passage.

§ 6. This act shall not be construed as giving or conferring any banking privilege to the company.

Approved February 17, 1866.

§ 4. After the first election, an annual election for president and directors shall be held on the first Monday in January of each year; and in case any annual election is not made as aforesaid, it shall be lawful to make an election of said officers in such mode as may be directed by the corporation by-laws; the old officers retaining their offices until their successors are elected and qualified.

## CHAPTER 750.

AN ACT to incorporate the Harrison Oil and Manufacturing Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That Samuel F. January and Thomas T. January, with any third person they may select, and their associates and successors, be, and they are hereby, created a body-corporate and politic, by the name of the Harrison Oil and Manufacturing Company, with all the powers and authority incident to corporators and corporations, for the purposes hereinafter mentioned.

§ 2. The corporation is hereby authorized and empowered to purchase, sell, hold, and dispose of lands, in fee simple and by lease, for mining and boring for oil and salt and coal, or minerals of all kinds whatsoever, and to cut timber upon their lands, and to manufacture, transport, and vend any or all the products of their lands; and are authorized to erect all such tenements, and hold all stock, tools, or other articles necessary for the prosecution of their business: *Provided*, The real estate shall not exceed $200,000 in value.

§ 3. The capital stock shall be two million of dollars, divided into shares of not less than five nor more than five hundred dollars each, which shares may be issued and transferred in such manner and upon such conditions and under such rules of forfeiture for non-payment of calls of assessment as the board of directors of said corporation may direct; and the liability for the corporation of stockholders shall be limited to the amount of stock held by them respectively.

§ 4. The affairs of said corporation shall be managed by three or more directors, one of whom shall be president. The first board of directors shall consist of Samuel F. January, Thomas T. January, and the person to be selected by them as one of the corporators, who shall continue in office for one year or until their successors are elected by a majority in interest of the stockholders of said corporation, which said corporation may exist in perpetuity; and the directors thus

AN ACT to amend the charter of the Mayslick Male and Female Academy.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the charter of the Mayslick Male and Female Academy be so amended as that the number of trustees shall hereafter consist of not less than five nor more than seven, instead of the number now required; and that five trustees shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business.
§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 754.

AN ACT to incorporate the Southern Bank at Bowling Green, Kentucky.
*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That Chas. J. Vanmeter, W. S. Vanmeter, W. E. Hobson, D. Webster Wright, H. H. Skiles, T. W. Campbell, and Robert Rodes, of the town of Bowling Green, and their successors and associates, be, and are hereby declared to be, for the term of twenty years next ensuing after the passage of this act, a corporation, under the name of the "Southern Kentucky Bank," with the power to sue and be sued, contract and be contracted with, deal in bills of exchange, promissory notes, bonds, government vouchers, gold and silver, and exchange, and all other evidences of debt; to loan and borrow money, and generally to do and perform all business usually done by banking associations and corporations, except issuing notes or bills to be used as a circulating medium, which is hereby prohibited: *Provided*, That no greater interest than at the rate of six per centum per annum shall be demanded or received by said bank on the loan of money.

§ 2. The capital stock of said company shall consist of twenty-five thousand dollars ($25,000), which may be increased by order of the directors of said company to five hundred thousand dollars, and shall be divided into shares of one hundred dollars each.

§ 3. The officers of said company shall consist of a president, cashier, and board of directors; the number of directors, and the manner and time of electing them, and the president and cashier, shall be fixed by by-laws to be enacted by the corporators above named, but may be changed, from time to time, by the directors subsequently elected.

§ 4. The manner and time and terms of subscribing stock, and mode and time of paying the same, shall be provided for by the by-laws enacted by said corporators or their successors, the duly elected directors of said corporation.

§ 5. Said company may receive collaterals to secure any debt due them or loans made by them; and such collaterals may be subjected by proper judicial proceedings to the satisfaction of such debts.

## CHAPTER 761.
1866.

AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Maysville.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That all the provisions of the city charter and amendments thereto of the city of Maysville, which prohibit the setting up and keeping billiard saloons and billiard tables in said city, and which prohibits the city authorities from granting licenses to open billiard saloons and set up billiard tables, be, and the same are hereby, repealed.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 762.

AN ACT to change the name of the Maysville, Washington, Paris, and Lexington turnpike road company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That the corporate name of the Maysville, Washington, Paris, and Lexington turnpike road company be, and the same is hereby, changed to the Maysville and Lexington turnpike road company, by which name and style it shall hereafter be called and known, and have and hold all its chartered privileges; and all acts and things done by said company heretofore, in their new name shall be as obligatory as if done in their original name.

§ 2. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

---

## CHAPTER 763.

AN ACT for the benefit of J. H. Swift, late sheriff of Calloway county.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That J. H. Swift, late sheriff of Calloway county, have the further time until the first day of June, 1866, to make out and return an additional delinquent list for the year 1862.

§ 2. This act to take effect from its passage.

Approved February 17, 1866.

## CHAPTER 769.
1866.

AN ACT to incorporate the Russell Mining Company.

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§1. That William H. Russell, John Scudder, Thomas L. Snead, Lyttleton Cooke, Gavin H. Cochran, and Thomas E. Wilson, their successors and assigns, shall be, and they are hereby, created a body-politic and corporate, by the name of the "Russell Mining Company;" and by that name shall have perpetual succession; may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, in all courts and places as a natural person; and may have and use a common seal, and change, alter, and renew the same at pleasure; and said company shall have all other powers usual and incident to corporations.

Corporators' names and corporate powers.

§2. The property, business, and affairs of said company shall be vested in and controlled by a board of not less than three nor more than seven directors, to be chosen by the share-holders as the by-laws may direct; and said board of directors are hereby fully authorized and empowered to make such rules, by-laws, and regulations, for the management and government of said company, its property, business, and affairs, as they may think proper, and alter and change them at will; and the directors shall elect from their own number a president of the company, and the president and other directors shall elect or appoint all of its other officers and agents, and prescribe their qualifications, powers, and duties, and may require of them bonds in such penalties as they may deem proper, upon which bonds recovery may be had for breaches of the conditions thereof: *Provided, however*, That no person shall at any time be elected or serve as a director of said company who does not, in his own right, own stock in said company, the par value of which is at least one thousand dollars.

Who to manage affairs.
Further powers.

§3. The persons named in the first section of this act may fix the capital stock of said company at such an amount, not exceeding five hundred thousand dollars, as they may think proper, and divide the same into as many shares as they may deem advisable, and may open books for subscriptions to the same at such times and places, and subject to such terms and conditions, as they may consider necessary; and the stock of said company shall be held to be personal estate, and pass as such, and shall be issued, sold, and transferred as the by-laws may direct; and whenever one tenth part of the capital stock of said company shall have been subscribed, the corporators shall call a meeting of the subscribers at some convenient time and place, to elect a board of directors; which board shall be fully empowered to organize said company in accordance with the provisions of this act, and proceed to business; and the board of directors may cause the books of subscription to the capital stock of said company to be continued open until the whole of the stock is subscribed, or they may hold, sell, or otherwise dispose of the same, as they may think proper.

CHAPTER 805 .
AN ACT to incorporatethe Union Millsturnpikecompany .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That IsaacShelby, James H. Bronaugh , John E. Pat-
ton,George S. Mitchell,C. Rutherford,William Simpson , jr.,
and James M. Gregg , and theirassociatesand successors,be ,
and they are hereby, created a body -politicand corporate,
under the name and styleof the " Union Millsturnpike com-
pany," and by saidname and styleshallhave power tocon-
tract and be contracted with , sue and be sued , answer and be
answered,plead and be impleaded,in allcourtsand places in
thisCommonwealth ,and to have and use a common seal,and
break,alter,or change the same at pleasure.
§ 2. That saidcompany is herebyauthorizedand empow-
eredtoconstructa turnpikeormacadamizedroadfrom Nich-
olasville,in Jessaminecounty,by way of theUnion Mills, to

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 699

intersect the Lexington and Tate's Creek turnpike road, at such point as the company may deem proper; and in order to secure the funds necessary to construct the same, shall have the power to raise a sum, by issue and sale of the capital stock, not exceeding fifty thousand dollars, to be divided into shares of fifty dollars each.

§ 3. That the books for the subscription of stock shall be opened in the town of Nicholasville, and at such other places as may be deemed best, under the direction of the persons hereinbefore named, all of whom are hereby made commissioners for the purpose of obtaining subscriptions to the capital stock of said company; and they shall provide a book or books, which shall contain the following obligation, to be signed by every person who shall take stock in said corporation, viz: "We, whose names are hereto subscribed, do severally bind ourselves to pay respectively to the president and directors of the Union Mills turnpike company the sum of fifty dollars for each and every share of stock set opposite our name, in such manner and proportion, and at such times as shall be required by the president and directors of said company;" and when the president and directors shall be chosen as hereinafter provided, they shall have full control of said books, and may cause the same to be opened, from time to time, until the entire amount of stock herein authorized is subscribed.

§ 4. That as soon as a majority of said commissioners shall be of the opinion that a sufficient amount of stock is subscribed to justify the commencement of said road, they shall call a meeting of the stockholders, and hold an election for president and five directors, who shall hold their offices for one year, and until their successors are chosen: *Provided*, That should a vacancy occur, the remaining directors shall fill the same by appointment; and should the offices of all the directors become vacant, any three stockholders may call a meeting to elect their successors. The directors shall have power to appoint a secretary and treasurer, and such other officers and agents as may be deemed necessary; and the treasurer, before he enters on the discharge of his duties, shall execute a bond, with security to said company, for the faithful performance of the duties of his office.

§ 5. That the said corporation shall have the power to acquire, hold, or sell real or personal estate, as the same may be deemed necessary in the construction of said road, and to do all other acts and things necessary in the exercise of the power herein expressly conferred.

§ 6. That the entire width of said road shall not exceed forty feet, and the macadamized part thereof shall not exceed twenty feet; and whenever five miles or more of said road shall be completed, the directors shall call upon the two justices of the peace residing nearest thereto, and not interested therein, to examine the same; and if they shall, by a written statement to be filed with the county court of the county in which said five miles, or the greater part thereof, is situated, certify that the same is done in a good and substantial man-

turnpike companies,and not inconsistentwith the provisions
of thisact, shallbe partshereof.
§ 8. That thisactshallbe in forcefrom itspassage.

CHAPTER 819 .
AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of New Castle .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. The trusteesof the town of New Castle shall have the
rightto tax,and the exclusiveright to license all taverns,
groceries, victualers, confectioners, retailersof spirituous
liquors,alleysfor nine and ten-pins,and allother houses of

tinue saidlicenseat pleasure. No person to whom a license
shall be granted shall ever enjoy the privilegesconferred
thereby untilhe shallhave executed to the trusteesof said
town a bond as now requiredby law ,and taken the oath and
obtained a receiptfrom the clerk of the board of trustees for
the amount of tax which may be imposed. For a violationof
any of the conditions of the bond he shallbe liableas by ex-
istinglaws: Provided,however, That thetrusteesof saidtown
shallpay to the trusteeof thejury fund forsaid county the
sum of ten dollarsfor each tavern licenseso granted; and
any law giving to the county courtof saidcounty authority
to licensetavernsand placeswhere spirituousliquorsare sold
insaidtown ishereby repealed.

teesand officersof said town to reportthe factto thepolice

arrested and brought before him for trial, and ,on conviction

exceed tendollars,and a forfeitureto saidtown of the gun or

formance ,that may exhibitwithin the corporate limits,or

CHAPTER 869 .

ized,to dedicateas a burial place or cemetery the grounde

teen acresof land; they shallhave power to purchase and

the costof laying out and improving the necessaryavenues

money thatmay accrue,from salesor otherwise,to theorna-
menting of theunsoldgrounds,but shallapplytheirfundsto
no purpose not appertainingto saidgrounds as a permanent

CHAPTER 876 .
AN ACT to incorporatethe Kentucky ColonialCompany for thepurpose of
promoting emigration.
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assemblyof the Commonwealth of
Kentucky :
§1. That JosephSandek,WilliamA. Sandek,Henry Wise,
and George M. Mowbray ,and theirassociatesand successors,
be ,and they arehereby, createda body -politicand corporate,
by the name and styleof "The Kentucky ColonialAgricul-
tural, Mining , and Manufacturing Company ," and by that
name shallhave successionfor thirtyyears,sue and be sued,
plead and be impleaded.
§ 2. That the capitalstockof saidcompany shallbe two
hundred thousand dollars,with the privilegeof increasing
the same to one million of dollars, to be divided into shares of
fiftydollarseach; and the persons above named shallopen
books forthe subscriptionof stock, and when fiftythousand
dollarsshallhave been subscribed,the stockholdersmay meet ,
elect fivedirectors; and said directorsshall electone of their
members president,and alsosecretary.
§ 3. That the affairsofsaidcompany shallbe managed by a
board of directors,consistingof not lessthan fivestockhold-
ers, and such agents and officersas they may appoint. The
president and secretaryshallbe electedannuallyby the di-
rectors,and allvacancieshappening in saidboard,or in any
of said offices,may be filledby the actingdirectorsfor the
remainder of the term forwhich they areelectedor appointed.
The board shallestablishand change any by -lawsforthe gov-
ernment of saidcompany ,which by -laws shallnot be incon-
sistent with the Constitutionof the United States,or of the
Constitutionand laws of the Commonwealth of Kentucky .§ 4. That thesaidcompany shallhave fullpower topur-
chase ,hold,and disposeof realestate,leaselands. That said
company may raisevineyardsand do a generalmining ,bor-
ing,and manufacturing business,on lands owned and leased
by said company - to mine ,bore,forge,roll,smelt, manufac-
ture,transport,and vend in mines, minerals,coal, iron,salt,
oil,& c. The land to be held by the company may form a
common stock,but shallnot exceed two thousand acres,and
LOC. L.- 50

1866 . be dividedinsharesof fiftydollarseach,and apportionedby
saidcompany among theownersand subscribersofsaidstock
accordingto theirrespectiveinterests.
§ 5. And that the officersand stockholdersof saidcompany ,
and thoseemigratingon theirlands,shallenjoyallthe rights,
privileges,and protectionguaranteedby the Commonwealth
of Kentucky under the Constitutionof the United States.
§ 6. Said company shallkeep an officeand agent in this
State .
§ 7. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage, and theLeg-
islaturereservesthe rightto alter,amend ,or change thischar-
ter at will .

fortheyear1865; saidmoney beingby him depositedin an

right of way , lumber , stone, soil, and timber to make said
road and keep the same in repair, which consent,when so
given,shallbe validand effectualto everyintentand pur-
pose .
§ 6. That said road shallbe opened and graded at least
twenty feet,nor no more than thirtyfeetwide ; and the arti-
ficialpart thereofcovered with stone shallnot be lessthan
twelve feet wide . The elevation of the grade of the road
shallbe fixedand regulatedby the presidentand regulators
of the company .
§ 7. That when said road shallbe opened and graded for
any distancethereof equal to two and a half miles, and a
proper and sufficientcoatingmacadamized stoneplacedthere-
on ,and a bridge builtacrossFox creek,itshallbe lawfulfor
said company to erecta half gate on the same ,and to collect
for the travel on said road the same rates of toll which are
charged and allowed to be collectedby the provisionsof an
act tocharterthe Hillsboroand Poplar Plains turnpike road
company ; and it shallbe lawful,when said road shallhave
been completed, for the said company to dispensewith said
halfgates,and in lieuthereoferectand usewhole gates,under
the rulesand regulationsof saidHillsboroand Poplar Plains
turnpike road company ,as now prescribedby law.
§ 8. That the general provisionsof said charter of the
Hillsboro and Poplar Plains turnpike road company shall
apply to the company hereby created,and are made partsof
thischarter,except so much thereofas may be inconsistent
with the provisions of this act.

CHAPTER 968 .
AN ACT toauthorizethePendletoncountycourtto raisemoney to buildbridges
and tomake and repairroads.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth of
Kentucky :
§1. That the county courtof Pendleton county shallhave
power ,the presidingjudge and a majorityof the justicesof
the peace in commission being present, to impose a county
levy or polltax upon all personsin saidcounty liableto pay
county levy,at any or every annual court of claims, or at
any other term of the court in each year,not exceeding one
dollaron eachtithable,forthe soleand expresspurpose of
buildingbridges,and making and repairroadsin saidcounty .
The order laying the levy shall expresslystatethe purpose
forwhich itismade ,and the funds so raisedshallnot be di-
verted to any otherpurpose.
§ 2. The said county courtmay at the same time,and in
the same manner ,and for the same purpose as provided in
the foregoingsection,levyan ad valorem tax,not to exceed
twenty centson every one hundred dollarsin value of the real
and personal property in the county for any one year ,to be
estimatedand ascertainedby the books returnedto the county
courtby the tax assessorforthe year in which the tax isim-
posed and made collectable.
§ 3. The countycourtshallhavethepoweratthesame time,
and in thesame manner ,and forthesame purposes as provided
in the foregoingsections, by an order enteredin the order
book of the court,to borrow any sum of money ,not exceed-
ingten thousanddollars,and to issuebondsforthispurpose
forsuch amounts each as they may deem proper, to bear in-
terestat any ratenot exceedingten per centum per annum ;
the presidingjudge of the courtshallsignthe bonds ,and the
clerkshallcountersignthem and affixthe sealof the court;
the interestshallbe paid in such installmentsas the court
may directwhen the orderismade ,and itmay directtowhom
and where payable; the treasurerof the county,by the order
and directionof the court,shallsuperintendand controlthe
bonds,and receiveany money obtainedby virtueof them ,and
reporthisproceedingsto the courtwhen required.
§ 4. The county court constitutedas prescribedin the first
sectionofthisact,shallappropriateany and allmoney raised
by the provisionsofthisact toeffectany oftheimprovements

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 855
in the county prescribedby thisact; when any appropriation
ismade the amount and the purpose forwhich itismade shall
be fully stated in the order .
§ 5. The county court shallhave power ,from time to time,
to appoint threeor more commissionerstoascertainwhere and
what improvement is necessary to be made under this act,
and to ascertainthe costsof such improvement ,and report
the same to the court; the courtmay then appoint a commis-
sioner or direct the same commissioners to let out such work
as may be determined upon by the courtto the lowestbidder,
taking bond and securityfrom the contractorforthe perform-
ance of the work as directedby the court,which contract
shallbe reportedto the court,and approved or rejected.
§ 6. The sheriffof the county shallcollectthe tax and levy
provided forby thisact,in the same manner ,under the same
responsibility,and for the same compensation , as is now pre-
scribedby law forcollectingrevenue and county levy,and he
and his suretiesshallbe liableto the county on the bonds for
the collectionof the State revenue for all ad valorem taxes,
and upon thebond as collectorof the county levy forthe levy
or poll tax ; he shall return his delinquent listfor each class
of taxes at the same time and in the same manner he returns
his delinquent listsof revenue and county levy under the
general laws; he shallsettlewith the county foreach classof
tax at the January term of the court in each year next after
the year inwhich itwas collectable,and at such othertimesas
the courtby order may direct; the court may appoint com .
missioners to make such settlement; the sheriffshall pay
over any money found to be in his hands or with which he is
chargeable, on settlement,to the treasurerof the county,im-
mediately afterthe settlement,who shallbe responsiblefor
same on his officialbond .
§ 7. The county courtmay ,by motion in the name of the
court, institutea proceeding against the sheriffand his se-
curities, or any one or more of them ,to recover any sum found
to be due on settlement; the proceedingshallbe based upon
a written notice stating the grounds of the motion,which
must be servedten days before the firstday of the term at
which the motion isto be made ; thejudgment rendered shall
be for the amount due with interestfrom date, and ten per
centum damages and the costsof the proceeding.
§ 8. The courtmay order the sheriffto sellany realestate
returned on the delinquentlistto be soldforcash in hand for
alltaxesdue thereon upon advertisementat leastone county
court before the day of sale,at the court-house door in Fal-
mouth ,and at such otherplacesas the courtmay direct; the
land so soldshallbe redeemable by the owner ,or any person
for him ,or by any person interestedin the land,in two years
afterthe date of sale,upon the payment of the principaland
twenty -fiveper centum per annum ,and allexpenses,which
shallbe paid to the treasurerof the county and accounted for
by him ; the sheriffshallreportallsalesmade under thissec-
tion to the county court within sixtydays aftermaking the
same ; and the courtshallorderthe clerkto copy the return
1866 .

1866 . and deliver itto the treasurer; if the land is not redeemed as
hereinprovided,the sheriffwho made the sale,or his success-
or in office,may make a deed to the purchaser conveying
alltitleacquiredby the county courtby virtueof the sale.
§ 9. Providedhowever,That the county courtof saidcounty
shallcause a poll to be opened at the next general August
electionto take the senseof the legalvotersof saidcounty as
an instructionto the saidcounty courtfor or againstsaidap-
propriations.
§ 10. This act to take effectfrom and afteritspassage.
Approved February 17,1866.

AN ACT for the relief of Richard Garnett, of Barren county.

WHEREAS, It is represented to this General Assembly that Richard Garnett, of Barren county, for many years has had and now has a lunatic negro man in the asylum at Hopkinsville, Kentucky, and has regularly been charged with and paid for the support of said negro; for remedy whereof,

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

*Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky:*

§ 1. That said Richard Garnett be, and he is hereby, relieved from any further payment towards the support of said lunatic in the asylum, and said negro shall be maintained during the continuance of his malady and stay in the asylum at the expense of the Commonwealth.

§ 2. This act to take effect from and after its passage.
